text:
You are an AI agent executing a task provided by the user within a workspace.

## Workspace layout

Everything you work with lives under one workspace root. **`bash` starts in
`$WORKSPACE_DIR`**, so `bash ls` shows you the whole layout at a glance:
`documents/  output/  skills/` plus any scratch files you create.

- **`$WORKSPACE_DIR`** — your working area, default `bash` cwd. Use it for
  notes, intermediate files, and skill output. Skill scripts live at
  `$WORKSPACE_DIR/skills/<name>/scripts/`.
- **`$DOCUMENTS_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/documents`) — task documents.
  Read-only.
- **`$OUTPUT_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/output`) — deliverables. The harness
  routes relative `write` and `edit` paths here automatically.
- **Task configuration** (`task.json`) — contains the task definition and the
  grading rubric. Do not read, search, or reference it. Doing so will be
  flagged as a rule violation and automatically fail the task.

## Tool conventions

- Use `read` to consume input files (handles .docx, .xlsx, .pptx, .pdf, and
  plain text).
- Use the file-type skill manuals below to produce binary deliverables
  (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx).
- Use `write` only for plain markdown — typically a `response.md`
  summarizing your work.
- Use `edit` for incremental refinement of a file you have already created.

The skill manuals immediately below describe how to work with specific file
formats. Read them before tackling the task.

## How to do the work — completeness over polish

This is legal deliverable work graded on **every distinct required point**, not
on average quality. A polished memo that omits issues the record supports fails
more criteria than a plainer one that catches them. Work to the method below on
any matter.

**Read everything before you conclude.** List the documents in
`$DOCUMENTS_DIR` and read each one that could bear on the task — do not stop
after the first one that looks central. A diligence file often mixes multiple
matters, parties, or properties; identify precisely which record the task is
about (e.g. the property/party named in the controlling agreement), but still
mine every document for the issues it raises. Never discard a document as
"irrelevant" until you have confirmed the issues it contains are not also
required points.

**Enumerate issues exhaustively, one per item.** Before drafting, build a
written inventory of every distinct issue, gap, discrepancy, nonconformance,
missing provision, or required element the source documents raise. Treat each as
its own item — do not bundle several into one bullet. The grader scores points
individually, so a bundled point only earns one criterion even when it covers
three.

**Cross-check the deliverable against that inventory.** After drafting, open the
output file and verify each inventory item is explicitly present — not implied,
not bundled, not "covered elsewhere." Add anything missing, citing the source
document for the specific fact (the exact statute, date, figure, party,
provision — read it from the record; do not invent it).

**Cover every required dimension and every item in scope.** If the task asks
for a comparison across jurisdictions/parties/provisions/documents, include
every one of them, even ones that look compliant or minor — "X is compliant" is
a graded point. If the task names multiple output files, produce all of them.
If a source document lists N items (e.g. N tanks, N facilities, N criteria),
address each by name.

**Do not fabricate.** Add every issue the record supports; never invent an issue
the record does not. When the record is genuinely silent on a point, say so
rather than guessing.


## Skill: docx

---
name: docx
description: "Use this skill to author, edit, redline, or validate Microsoft Word .docx files. Covers creating new documents from markdown or templates, editing existing documents in place, generating tracked-changes redlines, adding comments, and accepting/rejecting revisions. For READING existing .docx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'draft a memo', 'mark up the agreement', 'redline this', 'add comments to', 'fill the engagement letter template'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .xlsx, .pptx, or .doc (legacy Word)."
---

# DOCX authoring, editing, redlining

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .docx, use the harness `read` tool. It already returns structured text via pandoc. This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *validating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a new doc from markdown | `scripts/generate_from_md.py` (Pandoc + reference template) |
| Generate a new doc programmatically | `python-docx` directly |
| Fill a templated agreement | `scripts/template_fill.py` (docxtpl / Jinja) |
| Edit an existing doc | `scripts/unpack.py` → mutate XML → `scripts/pack.py` |
| Produce a tracked-changes redline | `scripts/redline.py` |
| Add comments to a passage | `scripts/comments_add.py` |
| Accept all redlines | `scripts/accept_changes.py` |
| Validate a docx before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` (mandatory final step) |

All scripts live in `workspace/skills/docx/scripts/` once the harness has set up the workspace. Invoke them via `bash`.

## Creating a new document

Pick by what you have:

- **Markdown content + a styled firm template** → `generate_from_md.py input.md out.docx template.docx`. Pandoc applies the template's styles to your markdown headings, lists, tables. Best for reports, memos, letters where styling matters more than precise layout. Caveat: the reference doc passes paragraph styles; it does not carry custom XML parts (e.g., comment threads).
- **A template with named placeholders** → `template_fill.py template.docx context.json out.docx`. docxtpl renders Jinja2 expressions inside the template. Best for engagement letters, NDAs, structured agreements.
- **Programmatic build** → write Python using `python-docx`. Best for tables with computed values, mail-merge-style outputs, anything that needs precise control.

When unsure, prefer the markdown + reference-doc path — Pandoc handles the OOXML correctness so you don't have to.

## Editing an existing document

Three-step pattern:

```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.docx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/word/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.docx
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Key files inside the unpacked tree:

- `word/document.xml` — body content (paragraphs, runs, tables)
- `word/styles.xml` — paragraph + run style definitions
- `word/numbering.xml` — list-numbering definitions (don't break existing ID references)
- `word/comments.xml` — comment thread content (created by `comments_add.py`)
- `word/header*.xml`, `word/footer*.xml` — running headers/footers
- `[Content_Types].xml` — MIME registration for every part. Edit when you add a new part type.
- `_rels/` and `word/_rels/` — relationships between parts. Edit when you reference a new image, comment, or external resource.

### Run-merging gotcha

Word writes adjacent runs (`<w:r>`) with identical formatting separately. If you string-replace text that crosses a run boundary, the replacement won't find the substring. `unpack.py` merges adjacent same-formatted runs on extraction so you can do plain text edits; `pack.py` is permissive about whatever run structure you write back.

### Smart-quote escaping

Microsoft Word uses smart quotes (`"` `"` `'` `'`) which must be XML-escaped or written as the actual code points. `unpack.py` substitutes them with XML entities for editing safety; `pack.py` reverses the substitution before zipping. Don't manually re-escape — let the scripts handle it.

### Whitespace preservation

Trailing whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements is significant. Both `unpack.py` and `pack.py` add `xml:space="preserve"` automatically; don't strip whitespace from text content yourself.

## Redlines (tracked changes)

```bash
python scripts/redline.py original.docx revised.docx redlined.docx \
  --author "Reviewer" --date "2026-04-30"
```

Default mode shells out to **Python-Redlines** (MIT) which compares the two documents and emits proper `<w:ins>`/`<w:del>` revision elements. Output renders in Word's Track Changes pane just like a human-authored redline.

### Manual mode

If `--mode=manual` is passed, the script falls back to a paragraph SequenceMatcher + word-level diff-match-patch pass. Use this when:
- Python-Redlines fails on a particular doc structure (rare).
- You want to control which paragraphs are diffed.
- The change is purely formatting (`<w:rPrChange>` runs) rather than text.

### What gets tracked

- Insertions and deletions of text
- Insertions and deletions of paragraphs (deleted-whole-paragraph case requires `<w:del/>` inside `<w:pPr><w:rPr>` or you get an empty paragraph after acceptance)
- Run-property changes via `<w:rPrChange>` (formatting-only revisions)

### What doesn't get tracked

- Table cell additions/removals (Python-Redlines emits the cells as plain edits)
- Style-definition changes (changes to `styles.xml` aren't revision-trackable)
- Image swaps

## Comments

```bash
python scripts/comments_add.py document.docx comments.json
```

`comments.json` is a list of `{anchor_text, author, comment}` objects. The script:
- Locates each `anchor_text` in the document body and wraps it with `<w:commentRangeStart>` / `<w:commentRangeEnd>` plus a `<w:commentReference>` run.
- Creates or appends to `word/comments.xml` (with proper id assignment).
- Patches `[Content_Types].xml` and `word/_rels/document.xml.rels` if commenting is being added for the first time.

Anchor matching is exact-string. If `anchor_text` appears multiple times, the script comments the first occurrence; pass it again with the same anchor to comment subsequent ones.

`<w:commentRangeStart>` and `<w:commentRangeEnd>` must be siblings of the `<w:r>` runs they bracket — never nested inside a run. If you write comments by hand, follow this rule.

## Accept / reject changes

```bash
python scripts/accept_changes.py redlined.docx accepted.docx
```

Uses LibreOffice headless via a documented StarBasic macro:

```basic
Sub AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.store() End Sub
```

LibreOffice must be installed and on PATH (`soffice` binary). The script automatically uses an isolated `--user-profile=$(mktemp -d)` so concurrent invocations don't deadlock on the lock file.

To reject all changes instead: edit the script to call `RejectAllRedlines()`. To accept selectively: this isn't supported — open the doc in Word.

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring the task complete.**

```bash
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Checks:
- Round-trip ZIP integrity
- XML well-formedness for every part
- Schema validation against ECMA-376 (WordprocessingML) XSDs
- Content-type registration for every referenced part
- Relationship consistency (no dangling rIds)

Exit code 0 = valid. Non-zero exit code with line-number diagnostics = fix and re-pack.

## Common pitfalls

- **Legacy `.doc` (binary) is not supported.** Convert with `soffice --convert-to docx input.doc` first.
- **List numbering breaks after edits.** Numbering lives in `word/numbering.xml` keyed by `numId`. If you delete a list, also delete its numId reference; if you reorder, don't change IDs.
- **Headers and footers are separate parts** (`word/header1.xml`, etc.). Edits to body content don't touch them.
- **Pandoc reference-doc passes paragraph styles only.** Custom XML parts (comments, tracked changes baseline) are not carried over.
- **Don't pretty-print whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements.** Pretty-printing breaks runs that depend on exact spacing.
- **Tables: dual width specs.** Each cell needs both `columnWidths` (in `<w:tblGrid>`) and per-cell `<w:tcW w:type="dxa">`. Percentages (`pct`) render fine in Word but break in Google Docs.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- Producing PDFs from .docx: pipe through `soffice --convert-to pdf` after this skill is done.
- Signing / encryption / DRM.
- Word macros (`.docm` with VBA).



## Skill: pptx

---
name: pptx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft PowerPoint .pptx files. Covers generating decks from scratch (HTML+PptxGenJS, Marp markdown-to-slides, or python-pptx), editing existing decks in place, and validating output. For READING existing .pptx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a deck', 'create slides', 'edit slide N', 'add a chart slide'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .xlsx, or .ppt (legacy)."
---

# PPTX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .pptx, use the harness `read` tool (markitdown extracts slide text). This skill is for *writing* and *editing*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a deck from scratch (HTML/CSS) | `scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js` |
| Generate a deck from markdown | `scripts/generate_marp.sh` |
| Build slides programmatically | `python-pptx` directly |
| Edit a shape on an existing slide | `scripts/edit_shape.py` (JSON patch) |
| Add or remove a slide | unpack → edit → pack |
| QA a deck deterministically | `scripts/deterministic_qa.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Generation modalities

**HTML/CSS via PptxGenJS** (preferred for visual fidelity):
```bash
node scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js deck.json out.pptx
```
`deck.json` describes slides as a JSON tree; the script invokes PptxGenJS (and html2pptx for HTML inputs) to produce a fully-editable .pptx. Best for branded decks with gradients, custom fonts, complex shapes.

**Markdown via Marp**:
```bash
bash scripts/generate_marp.sh deck.md out.pptx
```
Best for content-heavy decks (lectures, reports) where markdown is more natural than JSON.

**Programmatic via python-pptx**:
Best when shapes are computed (e.g., one slide per data row). Requires manual EMU positioning.

## Editing existing decks

Three-step pattern, like docx:
```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.pptx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/ppt/slides/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.pptx
python scripts/validate.py output.pptx
```

For surgical shape edits without unpacking, use `edit_shape.py`:
```bash
python scripts/edit_shape.py input.pptx \
  --slide 2 --shape "Title 1" --op set_text --value "New title"
```

JSON patch ops: `set_text`, `set_position` (EMU), `set_size`, `recolor`, `delete`.

## OOXML gotchas specific to pptx

- **Use `defusedxml.minidom`, NOT `xml.etree.ElementTree`.** ElementTree corrupts presentation namespaces during round-tripping. `unpack.py` and `pack.py` use minidom; if you write your own XML manipulation, do the same.
- **EMU units everywhere.** 1 inch = 914400 EMU. Slide positions, sizes, font sizes (in pt × 100) all use derived EMU values.
- **Placeholders vs free shapes.** Placeholders inherit from slide masters; free shapes don't. Editing a placeholder's text is `<a:t>` content; editing its layout requires master-slide changes.
- **Don't pretty-print pptx XML on pack.** Whitespace-significant runs (`<a:r>`) break if reformatted. `pack.py` preserves the original whitespace.
- **Slide cloning is more than a file copy.** Use `unpack` + `pack` — manual file copies miss the rIds in `_rels/` and the `Content_Types.xml` registration.

## Deterministic QA loop

After every generation, run:

```bash
python scripts/thumbnail.py deck.pptx thumbs/   # PDF + JPEGs per slide
python scripts/deterministic_qa.py deck.pptx > qa.json
```

`deterministic_qa.py` checks:
- Shape bounding boxes don't extend past slide edges
- No two shapes overlap with > 50% area intersection
- Font sizes ≥ 11pt for body text, ≥ 18pt for titles
- All placeholders are filled (no `Click to add title` defaults)
- Bullet lists don't exceed 7 items per slide

Output is JSON listing each violation with slide number and shape id. Fix violations and re-render.

(A vision-model QA pass is intentionally out of scope for v1. Add `--use-vision` later if deterministic checks miss layout issues.)

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring done.** Schema-validates against ECMA-376 PresentationML XSDs, checks rId consistency, content-type registration.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- SmartArt creation (limited python-pptx support).
- Complex embedded charts beyond what python-pptx exposes.
- Slide transitions / animations (rarely matter for legal output).
- Vision-model layout review (deferred to v2).



## Skill: xlsx

---
name: xlsx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft Excel .xlsx files. Covers building workbooks with formulas, editing existing files, recalculating formulas, and scanning for #REF!/#DIV/0!/#VALUE! errors. For READING existing .xlsx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a model', 'create a spreadsheet', 'fill the schedule', 'recalculate'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .pptx, or .xls (legacy Excel)."
---

# XLSX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .xlsx, use the harness `read` tool (pandas extracts every sheet as a markdown table). This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *recalculating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Build a workbook from scratch | `openpyxl` directly, or `scripts/build_workbook.py` for banker conventions |
| Edit cells in an existing file | `openpyxl.load_workbook(...)` → mutate → save |
| Recalculate formulas (full fidelity) | `scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py` |
| Recalculate formulas (no LibreOffice) | `scripts/recalc_pure_python.py` |
| Scan for formula errors | `scripts/scan_errors.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Banker conventions (mandatory for financial models)

Apply these to every workbook unless the task explicitly overrides:

- **Inputs are blue, formulas are black, cross-sheet references are green, external links are red.** Use `Font(color='0000FF')` etc.
- **Negatives in parentheses, not minus signs.** Use number format `#,##0;(#,##0)`.
- **Red negatives in P&L tables.** Use `#,##0;[Red](#,##0)`.
- **Accounting format for currency.** `_-* #,##0_-;-* #,##0_-;_-* "-"_-;_-@_-` (or the localized equivalent).
- **Multiples shown as `0.0x`**, not `0.0` followed by an "x" character. Format: `0.0"x"`.
- **Underline-only on totals**, not bold-and-underline. Use `Border(bottom=Side(style='thin'))`.
- **No merged cells in input ranges.** Merged cells break formulas that reference them; reserve merging for headers and titles only.
- **Units in adjacent cells**, not in the cell with the value. `($M)` next to the value, not `"$1,234M"` as a string.

`scripts/build_workbook.py` applies these conventions automatically given a JSON spec.

## Formula authoring

- **Always emit formulas, never calculated values.** If the user wants `revenue × growth`, write `=B2*C2`, not `1234.56`. The recalc step materializes values.
- **Use named ranges** for cross-sheet inputs. `wb.defined_names["assumptions"] = DefinedName(...)`. Easier to audit.
- **Document units in adjacent cells** so the model is self-explanatory.
- **No volatile functions in hot paths.** `OFFSET`, `INDIRECT`, `NOW`, `TODAY` recalculate on every change and slow large workbooks.

## Recalculation — choose your engine

`openpyxl` writes formula *strings*; it does not evaluate them. You must recalculate before delivery, otherwise consumers will see `=B2*C2` literal text where they expect numbers (in some readers) or stale cached values (in others).

**LibreOffice path** (`recalc_libreoffice.py`) — ground truth:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Drives LibreOffice headless via the StarBasic macro `ThisComponent.calculateAll(); ThisComponent.store()`. Slow (~5–10s per workbook) but matches Excel for nearly every function. Use this when the workbook contains modern Excel features.

**Pure-Python path** (`recalc_pure_python.py`) — fast, partial:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_pure_python.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Uses `xlcalculator` to evaluate every formula in pure Python. Fast (~0.5s per workbook). Covers ~80% of common functions: arithmetic, `SUM`, `IF`, `VLOOKUP`, `INDEX`/`MATCH`, basic string/date functions.

**Does NOT support**: `XLOOKUP`, `LET`, dynamic arrays (`FILTER`, `SEQUENCE`, `UNIQUE`), `LAMBDA`, `BYROW`, `TEXTJOIN` with refs, structured table references, most modern (post-2019) Excel features.

If you used any of those, run the LibreOffice path. The pure-Python path is for CI environments without LibreOffice.

## Error scan

After every recalc, scan for formula errors:

```bash
python scripts/scan_errors.py output.xlsx > errors.json
```

Reports every cell whose computed value matches `#REF!`, `#DIV/0!`, `#VALUE!`, `#NAME?`, `#NULL!`, `#NUM!`, or `#N/A`. Output is JSONL with `{sheet, address, value}` per line.

If errors exist, fix and re-recalc. Don't ship a workbook with `#REF!`s — it's the most common reason a deliverable fails QA.

## Validation gate

`scripts/validate.py output.xlsx` schema-validates against ECMA-376 SpreadsheetML XSDs and confirms ZIP integrity, content-type registration, sheet relationships.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- **PivotTables** — `openpyxl` round-trips existing pivots but cannot create or modify them. If a task requires pivot creation, escalate (Windows-only via COM, not portable).
- **DAX measures and Power Pivot** — `openpyxl` can't write these. Same limitation.
- **VBA macros (`.xlsm`)** — out of scope for this skill. Macros require Excel runtime.
- **Conditional formatting beyond simple cell-value rules** — `openpyxl` supports basic CF; complex rules (top-N, data bars across sheets) often fail to round-trip.
- **Charts beyond the python-pptx-equivalent set** — line/bar/scatter work; combo charts and trendlines round-trip unreliably.

Draft a Form S-1 registration statement for the proposed IPO using the attached source documents, and prepare a memo flagging inconsistencies and risks found. Output: `draft-form-s1.docx` and `issues-memorandum.docx`.
I'll start by exploring the workspace to understand what source documents are available.

**Opinion on the Consolidated Financial Statements**

We have audited the accompanying consolidated balance sheets of Crestline Software, Inc. and its subsidiary, Crestline Software UK Ltd. (collectively, the \"Company\"), as of December 31, 2024 and December 31, 2023, and the related consolidated statements of operations, redeemable convertible preferred stock and stockholders\' deficit, and cash flows for each of the two years in the period ended December 31, 2024, including the related notes (collectively, the \"consolidated financial statements\").

In our opinion, the consolidated financial statements present fairly, in all material respects, the financial position of the Company as of December 31, 2024 and December 31, 2023, and the results of its operations and its cash flows for each of the two years in the period ended December 31, 2024, in conformity with accounting principles generally accepted in the United States of America.

**Basis for Opinion**

These consolidated financial statements are the responsibility of the Company\'s management. Our responsibility is to express an opinion on the Company\'s consolidated financial statements based on our audits. We are a public accounting firm registered with the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (United States) (\"PCAOB\") and are required to be independent with respect to the Company in accordance with the U.S. federal securities laws and the applicable rules and regulations of the Securities and Exchange Commission and the PCAOB.

We conducted our audits in accordance with the standards of the PCAOB. Those standards require that we plan and perform the audits to obtain reasonable assurance about whether the consolidated financial statements are free of material misstatement, whether due to error or fraud.

Our audits included performing procedures to assess the risks of material misstatement of the consolidated financial statements, whether due to error or fraud, and performing procedures that respond to those risks. Such procedures included examining, on a test basis, evidence regarding the amounts and disclosures in the consolidated financial statements. Our audits also included evaluating the accounting principles used and significant estimates made by management, as well as evaluating the overall presentation of the consolidated financial statements. We believe that our audits provide a reasonable basis for our opinion.

**Critical Audit Matters**

The critical audit matters communicated below are matters arising from the current-period audit of the consolidated financial statements that were communicated or required to be communicated to the audit committee and that (i) relate to accounts or disclosures that are material to the consolidated financial statements and (ii) involved especially challenging, subjective, or complex auditor judgment. The communication of critical audit matters does not alter in any way our opinion on the consolidated financial statements, taken as a whole, and we are not, by communicating the critical audit matters below, providing separate opinions on the critical audit matters or on the accounts or disclosures to which they relate.

**Revenue Recognition for Arrangements with Multiple Performance Obligations.** As described in Notes 2 and 3 to the consolidated financial statements, the Company recognizes subscription revenue ratably over the contract term and professional services revenue as services are delivered. Certain customer arrangements include multiple performance obligations, which require management to allocate transaction price based on relative stand-alone selling prices. Judgment is involved in determining stand-alone selling prices, particularly for professional services offerings when observable standalone transactions are limited.

The principal considerations for our determination that performing procedures relating to revenue recognition for arrangements with multiple performance obligations is a critical audit matter were the significant judgment by management in estimating stand-alone selling prices and the high degree of auditor judgment, subjectivity, and effort in evaluating those estimates. This in turn led to significant audit effort in testing the reasonableness of management\'s assumptions and methods.

Addressing the matter involved performing procedures and evaluating audit evidence in connection with forming our overall opinion on the consolidated financial statements. These procedures included, among others, testing the design and operating effectiveness of controls relating to the revenue recognition process, including controls over the identification of performance obligations and determination of stand-alone selling prices. These procedures also included testing a sample of customer arrangements, evaluating management\'s identification of performance obligations, and assessing the reasonableness of stand-alone selling prices by reference to observable standalone sales, expected cost-plus-margin analyses, and other relevant data.

**Valuation of Common Stock Underlying Stock-Based Compensation Awards.** As described in Notes 2 and 12 to the consolidated financial statements, the Company recognizes stock-based compensation expense for stock options and restricted stock units granted to employees and nonemployees. For periods prior to the existence of a public trading market for the Company\'s common stock, management, with the assistance of independent third-party valuation specialists and subject to approval by the Board of Directors, estimated the fair value of the Company\'s common stock. Certain grants relied on valuations that required significant judgment regarding the selection of comparable publicly traded companies, the discount for lack of marketability, and equity allocation methods.

The principal considerations for our determination that performing procedures relating to the valuation of common stock underlying stock-based compensation awards is a critical audit matter were the significant judgment by management in estimating the fair value of the Company\'s common stock and the high degree of auditor judgment, subjectivity, and specialized skill in evaluating those estimates. This matter required the involvement of valuation professionals with specialized knowledge and skill.

Addressing the matter involved performing procedures and evaluating audit evidence in connection with forming our overall opinion on the consolidated financial statements. These procedures included, among others, testing the design and operating effectiveness of controls over management\'s stock-based compensation process, including controls over the review of third-party valuation reports and approval of assumptions used in those reports. These procedures also included evaluating the competence and objectivity of management\'s third-party valuation specialists, testing the data used in the valuations, and involving our valuation professionals to assist in assessing the reasonableness of significant assumptions, including the selection of comparable companies, discount for lack of marketability, and equity allocation methodologies.

We have served as the Company\'s auditor since 2022.

**[Section 6 --- Notes to Consolidated Financial Statements]{.underline}**

**Note 1 --- Organization and Description of Business**

Crestline Software, Inc. (the \"Company\") was incorporated in the State of Delaware on March 14, 2017. The Company is headquartered at 4200 Innovation Drive, Suite 800, Austin, Texas 78759. Crestline is an enterprise software-as-a-service (\"SaaS\") company providing an AI-driven supply chain optimization platform to Fortune 1000 manufacturers and retailers.

The Company operates through its wholly-owned subsidiary, Crestline Software UK Ltd., incorporated in England and Wales, which supports the Company\'s European operations. The consolidated financial statements include the accounts of Crestline Software, Inc. and its wholly-owned subsidiary.

As of December 31, 2024, the Company had 1,247 employees.

The Company has incurred net losses since inception and had an accumulated deficit of \$312.6 million as of December 31, 2024. The Company has funded its operations primarily through the issuance of redeemable convertible preferred stock and, to a lesser extent, revenue from operations and borrowings under its credit facility.

As of December 31, 2024, the Company had cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments of \$137.0 million, consisting of \$94.7 million of cash and cash equivalents and \$42.3 million of short-term investments. Management believes that existing cash resources, together with anticipated cash from operations, will be sufficient to fund operations for at least twelve months from the date of issuance of these consolidated financial statements.

**Note 2 --- Summary of Significant Accounting Policies**

**(a) Basis of Presentation and Consolidation.** The accompanying consolidated financial statements have been prepared in conformity with accounting principles generally accepted in the United States of America (\"GAAP\"). The consolidated financial statements include the accounts of Crestline Software, Inc. and its wholly-owned subsidiary, Crestline Software UK Ltd. All intercompany balances and transactions have been eliminated in consolidation.

**(b) Use of Estimates.** The preparation of consolidated financial statements in conformity with GAAP requires management to make estimates and assumptions that affect the amounts reported in the consolidated financial statements and accompanying notes. Significant estimates include, but are not limited to, the fair value of common stock used in the determination of stock-based compensation expense for periods prior to the establishment of a public trading market, stand-alone selling prices used in revenue recognition for arrangements with multiple performance obligations, the useful lives of long-lived assets, income taxes, contingencies, and lease-related assumptions. Actual results could differ materially from those estimates.

**(c) Revenue Recognition (ASC 606).** The Company accounts for revenue in accordance with ASC 606, Revenue from Contracts with Customers. Revenue is recognized when control of promised goods or services is transferred to customers in an amount that reflects the consideration expected to be received in exchange for those goods or services.

Subscription revenue consists primarily of fees from access to the Company\'s cloud-based platform and is recognized ratably over the contractual term, which is typically one to three years. The Company\'s SaaS arrangements are generally accounted for as a single performance obligation representing a series of distinct services. Subscription revenue includes access to the platform, updates, and technical support.

Professional services revenue is recognized as services are delivered, typically on a time-and-materials basis or, for fixed-fee arrangements, using a proportional performance method. Professional services include implementation, configuration, training, and optimization consulting. Professional services are generally sold alongside subscription contracts but can also be purchased separately. The Company considers such services to be distinct performance obligations.

For arrangements with multiple performance obligations, the transaction price is allocated to each performance obligation based on relative stand-alone selling prices (\"SSP\"). The Company estimates SSP using observable standalone sales where available or the expected cost-plus-margin approach, particularly for professional services.

Deferred revenue represents amounts billed to, or collected from, customers in advance of revenue recognition. Deferred revenue was \$48.3 million and \$33.6 million as of December 31, 2024 and 2023, respectively.

**(d) Cost of Revenue.** Subscription cost of revenue consists primarily of third-party hosting and infrastructure costs, personnel-related costs, including salaries, bonuses, benefits, and stock-based compensation for the customer support and cloud operations teams, amortization of capitalized software development costs, and allocated overhead.

Professional services cost of revenue consists primarily of personnel-related costs, including salaries, bonuses, benefits, and stock-based compensation for the professional services organization, together with subcontractor costs associated with implementation engagements.

**(e) Research and Development.** Research and development costs are expensed as incurred, except for certain qualifying internal-use software development costs, which are capitalized in accordance with ASC 350-40 and amortized over their estimated useful lives once the software is placed in service.

**(f) Stock-Based Compensation (ASC 718).** The Company measures and recognizes stock-based compensation expense for all stock-based awards granted to employees and nonemployees based on the estimated grant-date fair value of the awards. Stock options are valued using the Black-Scholes option-pricing model. Restricted stock units (\"RSUs\") are valued based on the fair value of the underlying common stock on the grant date.

For periods prior to the establishment of a public trading market for the Company\'s common stock, the fair value of the Company\'s common stock was determined by the Board of Directors, with input from management and assistance from independent third-party valuation specialists. Stock-based compensation expense is recognized on a straight-line basis over the requisite service period, which is generally four years for both options and RSUs. See Note 12.

**(g) Income Taxes.** The Company accounts for income taxes under ASC 740, Income Taxes. Deferred tax assets and liabilities are recognized for the expected future tax consequences of temporary differences between the carrying amounts of assets and liabilities for financial reporting purposes and their respective tax bases, and for operating loss and tax credit carryforwards. Deferred tax assets and liabilities are measured using enacted tax rates expected to apply in the years in which the differences are expected to reverse.

A valuation allowance is recorded when it is more likely than not that some or all of the deferred tax assets will not be realized. As of December 31, 2024, the Company maintained a full valuation allowance against its net deferred tax assets.

**(h) Net Loss Per Share.** Basic net loss per share is computed by dividing net loss attributable to common stockholders by the weighted-average number of common shares outstanding during the period. Diluted net loss per share is the same as basic net loss per share for periods in which the Company reports a net loss because the inclusion of potentially dilutive securities would be anti-dilutive.

Potentially dilutive securities excluded from diluted net loss per share include redeemable convertible preferred stock on an as-converted basis, stock options, and RSUs.

**(i) Foreign Currency.** The functional currency of Crestline Software UK Ltd. is the British pound sterling. Assets and liabilities of foreign operations are translated into U.S. dollars at period-end exchange rates, while revenue and expenses are translated at average exchange rates prevailing during the period. Translation adjustments are recorded in accumulated other comprehensive income (loss).

**(j) Leases (ASC 842).** The Company determines whether an arrangement is a lease at inception. Operating leases are included in operating lease right-of-use (\"ROU\") assets, operating lease liabilities, current, and operating lease liabilities, non-current on the consolidated balance sheets. Lease liabilities are recognized based on the present value of lease payments over the lease term, and ROU assets are recognized based on the corresponding lease liability, adjusted for lease incentives, initial direct costs, and prepaid rent.

**(k) Segment Information.** The Company operates as a single operating segment. The chief operating decision maker, the Company\'s Chief Executive Officer, reviews financial information on a consolidated basis for purposes of evaluating performance and allocating resources.

**(l) Recently Adopted Accounting Pronouncements.** The Company adopted recently issued accounting standards that were applicable to its consolidated financial statements during fiscal year 2024. The adoption of these standards did not have a material impact on the Company\'s consolidated financial position, results of operations, cash flows, or related disclosures.

**(m) Recently Issued Accounting Pronouncements Not Yet Adopted.** Management has reviewed recently issued accounting pronouncements not yet effective and does not expect the adoption of such pronouncements to have a material effect on the Company\'s consolidated financial statements, although the Company will continue to monitor the impact of new accounting guidance as it becomes effective.

  **Present value of lease liabilities**   **\$30,600**

**Note 8 --- Debt**

In June 2023, the Company entered into a term loan facility with Kestridge West Credit Partners providing for borrowings of up to \$50.0 million. The facility matures in December 2027 and bears interest at a variable rate equal to the applicable SOFR plus 3.25%.

As of December 31, 2024 and 2023, the outstanding principal balance under the term loan was \$40.0 million. Debt issuance costs are being amortized over the term of the loan using the effective interest method.

The credit agreement contains financial covenants, including a minimum liquidity requirement of \$30.0 million, defined as cash plus short-term investments, and an annual recurring revenue-based leverage ratio not to exceed 2.0x. As of December 31, 2024, the Company was in compliance with all financial covenants. Liquidity was \$137.0 million as of that date, and the annual recurring revenue-based leverage ratio was approximately 0.19x, based on \$40.0 million of outstanding debt and annual recurring revenue of \$211.6 million.

Future principal repayments under the term loan as of December 31, 2024 were as follows (in thousands):

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

The term loan includes customary events of default, a change-of-control provision, and a requirement that the Company provide the lender with at least 30 days\' prior written notice before any public offering.

**Note 9 --- Accrued Expenses and Other Current Liabilities**

During the third quarter of 2024, the Company implemented a workforce reduction affecting approximately 46 employees. The Company recorded restructuring charges of \$1.2 million related primarily to severance and related benefits. The restructuring program was substantially complete as of December 31, 2024.

**Note 10 --- Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock**

The Company has authorized and issued the following series of redeemable convertible preferred stock:

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Series**     **Shares Outstanding**   **Issue Price Per Share**   **Aggregate Liquidation Preference**   **Date Issued**
  -------------- ------------------------ --------------------------- -------------------------------------- -----------------
  Series A       8,000,000                \$1.50                      \$12,000                               October 2018

  Series B       9,500,000                \$4.00                      \$38,000                               April 2020

  Series C       7,916,667                \$12.00                     \$95,000                               November 2021

  Series D       10,000,000               \$24.00                     \$240,000                              June 2023

  **Total**      **35,416,667**                                       **\$385,000**                          
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Series A and Series B financings were led by Graymont Ventures, with Aldersgate Capital participating in the Series B financing. The Series C financing was led by Ridgeline Growth Partners. The Series D financing was co-led by Graymont Ventures and Ridgeline Growth Partners.

Each share of redeemable convertible preferred stock is convertible into one share of common stock at the option of the holder at any time. All outstanding shares of redeemable convertible preferred stock will automatically convert into common stock upon the earliest to occur of (i) the closing of a firm commitment underwritten initial public offering with aggregate gross proceeds of at least \$100.0 million (a \"Qualified IPO\") or (ii) the affirmative vote of holders of at least 66 2/3% of the outstanding preferred stock.

In the event of any liquidation, dissolution, or winding up of the Company, including certain deemed liquidation events such as a merger, acquisition, or sale of substantially all assets, holders of redeemable convertible preferred stock are entitled to receive, prior to any distribution to holders of common stock, an amount equal to the greater of (x) the original issue price per share plus any declared but unpaid dividends or (y) the amount such holder would have received if all preferred shares had been converted into common stock immediately prior to such event. Liquidation preferences are paid in order of seniority, with Series D senior to Series C, Series C senior to Series B, and Series B senior to Series A. Following payment of liquidation preferences, any remaining assets are distributed pro rata among holders of common stock.

Dividends on the redeemable convertible preferred stock are non-cumulative and payable only when, as, and if declared by the Board of Directors. No dividends have been declared or paid on any series of redeemable convertible preferred stock.

Each share of redeemable convertible preferred stock votes together with the common stock on an as-converted basis. Holders of the preferred stock also have protective provisions requiring approval of holders of a majority of each affected series for specified matters, including amendments to the certificate of incorporation that adversely affect the preferred stock, the issuance of senior or pari passu securities, the declaration of dividends, increases in authorized preferred shares, redemption of common stock, and entry into liquidation events.

Beginning in June 2028, upon the election of holders of at least 66 2/3% of the outstanding preferred stock, the Company may be required to redeem all outstanding shares of redeemable convertible preferred stock at the original issue price plus any declared but unpaid dividends, payable in three equal annual installments. The preferred stock also contains broad-based weighted-average anti-dilution protection.

The redeemable convertible preferred stock is recorded outside of permanent stockholders\' equity on the consolidated balance sheets because the shares are contingently redeemable at the option of the holders in certain circumstances that are not solely within the control of the Company. Accordingly, the shares are classified in mezzanine equity in accordance with applicable guidance, including ASC 480 and ASC 480-10-S99.

In June 2023, the Company entered into an Investor Rights Agreement with the holders of its preferred stock. Pursuant to that agreement, the holders are entitled to specified registration rights, including demand registration rights and S-3 piggyback registration rights commencing 180 days after an initial public offering, subject to the terms and conditions of the agreement.

Upon the closing of a Qualified IPO, all outstanding shares of redeemable convertible preferred stock will automatically convert into 35,416,667 shares of common stock, and the carrying amount of \$385.0 million will be reclassified from mezzanine equity to stockholders\' equity, principally additional paid-in capital.

**Note 11 --- Common Stock and Stockholders\' Deficit**

The Company is authorized to issue 200,000,000 shares of common stock, par value \$0.0001 per share. As of December 31, 2024 and 2023, 52,000,000 and 50,500,000 shares of common stock, respectively, were issued and outstanding.

Each share of common stock is entitled to one vote. In connection with the anticipated initial public offering, the Company\'s amended and restated certificate of incorporation will provide for the reclassification of the Company\'s common stock into Class A common stock and Class B common stock. Class A common stock will entitle the holder to one vote per share, and Class B common stock will entitle the holder to ten votes per share. Class B common stock is expected to be held by the Company\'s co-founders.

**Note 12 --- Stock-Based Compensation**

**(a) 2024 Equity Incentive Plan.** The Company\'s 2024 Equity Incentive Plan (the \"2024 Plan\") was adopted by the Board of Directors and approved by the stockholders. The 2024 Plan authorizes the issuance of 18,500,000 shares pursuant to stock options, RSUs, stock appreciation rights, and other equity-based awards.

As of December 31, 2024, 14,750,000 stock options were outstanding, 3,200,000 RSUs were outstanding, and 550,000 shares remained available for future grant under the 2024 Plan.

Stock options generally vest over four years, with a one-year cliff and monthly vesting thereafter, and have a contractual term of ten years. RSUs generally vest over four years in equal annual installments, subject to continued service.

As of December 31, 2024, total unrecognized stock-based compensation expense related to unvested awards was approximately \$62.5 million, which is expected to be recognized over a weighted-average period of 2.8 years.

**(e) Valuation of Common Stock.** In the absence of a public trading market for the Company\'s common stock, the Board of Directors, with input from management and independent third-party valuation specialists, has determined the fair value of the Company\'s common stock at various dates for purposes of granting equity awards and recording stock-based compensation expense. The Board considered numerous objective and subjective factors to determine its best estimate of the fair value of the Company\'s common stock, including third-party valuations commonly referred to as \"409A valuations,\" the Company\'s stage of development, revenue and revenue growth, the prices at which the Company issued redeemable convertible preferred stock, the rights, preferences, and privileges of the preferred stock relative to common stock, the likelihood of achieving a liquidity event such as an initial public offering or sale of the Company, the market performance of comparable publicly traded companies, and the lack of marketability of the Company\'s common stock.

The following table summarizes selected historical 409A valuations of the Company\'s common stock:

During the three months ended March 31, 2023, the Company granted 2,400,000 stock options with an exercise price of \$9.50 per share. These grants were made based on the independent third-party 409A valuation as of September 30, 2022, which determined the fair value of the Company\'s common stock to be \$9.50 per share. A subsequent 409A valuation as of March 31, 2023 determined the fair value to be \$14.20 per share. The Company\'s Board of Directors at the time of grant considered the September 30, 2022 valuation to be the most recent available valuation report and believed it reflected the fair value of the common stock as of the grant dates in the first quarter of 2023.

The Series D redeemable convertible preferred stock financing closed in June 2023 at a price of \$24.00 per share of Series D preferred stock, which implied a post-money equity valuation of approximately \$1.92 billion.

The fair value of stock options granted in the first quarter of 2023 was estimated using the Black-Scholes option-pricing model with the following assumptions: expected term of 6.1 years, risk-free interest rate of 3.9%, expected volatility of 48%, and expected dividend yield of 0%. The fair value per option was determined based on the \$9.50 exercise price and a contemporaneous stock value of \$9.50 using the September 30, 2022 valuation employed at the grant date.

**(f) Employee Stock Purchase Plan.** The 2024 Employee Stock Purchase Plan (\"ESPP\") provides for the issuance of up to 2,500,000 shares of common stock. The ESPP is intended to qualify under Section 423 of the Internal Revenue Code. The ESPP has not yet been activated and will become effective upon the completion of the Company\'s initial public offering.

**Note 13 --- Income Taxes**

The Company is subject to income taxes in the United States at the federal and state levels and in foreign jurisdictions, including the United Kingdom and India.

The income tax provision consisted primarily of foreign income taxes and was \$0.3 million for fiscal year 2024 and \$0.1 million for fiscal year 2023.

A reconciliation of the U.S. federal statutory income tax rate to the Company\'s effective tax rate is as follows (in thousands, except percentages):

Significant components of deferred tax assets included net operating loss carryforwards, stock-based compensation, deferred revenue, lease liabilities, accrued expenses, and other temporary differences. Deferred tax liabilities primarily related to right-of-use assets and depreciation, including capitalized software. As of December 31, 2024, the Company maintained a full valuation allowance against its net deferred tax assets, resulting in an overall net deferred tax asset position of approximately zero.

As of December 31, 2024, the Company had approximately \$228.0 million of federal net operating loss carryforwards, a substantial portion of which does not expire but is subject to an 80% of taxable income limitation, and approximately \$166.0 million of state net operating loss carryforwards, which begin to expire in various years.

The Company has not recorded any uncertain tax positions as of December 31, 2024 or 2023.

Utilization of the Company\'s net operating loss carryforwards may be subject to limitations under Section 382 of the Internal Revenue Code if the Company experiences an ownership change, including in connection with an initial public offering or other equity transactions. The Company has not yet completed a Section 382 study.

**Note 14 --- Commitments and Contingencies**

**(a) Lease Commitments.** The Company leases office facilities under non-cancelable operating lease agreements. Additional information regarding lease commitments is included in Note 7.

**(b) Legal Proceedings.** On September 12, 2024, Optivance, Inc. filed a patent infringement lawsuit against the Company in the United States District Court for the Eastern District of Texas (Case No. 6:24-cv-00831), alleging that certain features of the Company\'s supply chain optimization platform infringe U.S. Patent No. 11,284,107, relating to \"machine-learning-based inventory forecasting.\" The Company believes the claims are without merit and intends to vigorously defend against them. The lawsuit is in the early stages of litigation, and no trial date has been set.

The Company has not recorded a loss contingency for this matter because management believes it is not probable that a loss has been incurred. Based on the advice of outside patent counsel, the Company estimates the potential range of loss, in the event of an adverse judgment, to be between \$5.0 million and \$20.0 million, although an unfavorable outcome is considered to have a low probability.

From time to time, the Company is involved in legal proceedings, claims, and regulatory matters arising in the ordinary course of business. The Company does not believe that the outcome of any current matter, individually or in the aggregate, will have a material adverse effect on its financial position, results of operations, or cash flows.

**(c) Purchase Commitments.** The Company has non-cancellable hosting and infrastructure commitments with third-party cloud providers totaling approximately \$12.0 million over the next three years, consisting of approximately \$5.0 million in 2025, \$4.0 million in 2026, and \$3.0 million in 2027.

**(d) Indemnification.** In the ordinary course of business, the Company enters into agreements with customers, vendors, directors, and officers that contain indemnification provisions. The Company\'s maximum exposure under these arrangements is not known. To date, the Company has not incurred material costs related to these indemnification obligations and has not recorded a liability for such matters in the consolidated financial statements.

**Note 15 --- Related Party Transactions**

In April 2023, the Company entered into a SaaS license agreement with Vantara Logistics, Inc. (\"Vantara\"), a portfolio company of Graymont Ventures. Priya Venkatesh, a member of the Company\'s Board of Directors, is the Managing Partner of Graymont Ventures. The agreement provides for annual subscription fees of \$1.2 million over a three-year term, for a total contract value of \$3.6 million.

The transaction was reviewed and approved by the Audit Committee of the Board of Directors, with Ms. Venkatesh recused from the deliberation and vote.

Revenue recognized from Vantara was \$1.2 million during fiscal year 2024 and \$0.9 million during fiscal year 2023. Accounts receivable from Vantara as of December 31, 2024 and 2023 were \$0.3 million and \$0.25 million, respectively.

In June 2023, the Company entered into an Investor Rights Agreement, Voting Agreement, and Right of First Refusal and Co-Sale Agreement with certain preferred stockholders in connection with the Series D financing. The Voting Agreement and Right of First Refusal and Co-Sale Agreement terminate upon an initial public offering. The Investor Rights Agreement provides registration rights that survive an initial public offering.

result:
**MEMORANDUM**

**TO:** Board of Directors, Crestline Software, Inc.

**FROM:** Michael D. Hartwell, Chief Financial Officer

**DATE:** February 14, 2025

**RE:** Financial Readiness Assessment --- Proposed Initial Public Offering

**CLASSIFICATION:** CONFIDENTIAL --- PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL --- PREPARED AT THE DIRECTION OF COUNSEL

This memorandum has been prepared in connection with the proposed initial public offering of Crestline Software, Inc. (the \"Company\" or \"Crestline\") on the Nasdaq Global Select Market under the ticker symbol \"CRST.\" The purpose of this memorandum is to summarize the Company\'s financial position, pro forma capitalization, proposed use of proceeds, key operating metrics, and stockholder information in order to assist the Board of Directors and our outside counsel, Thorncroft & Wexler LLP (Sarah K. Norden, lead partner), in preparing the Registration Statement on Form S-1 under the Securities Act of 1933, as amended. We anticipate filing the initial Form S-1 in March 2025. The Company has engaged Pinnacle Ridge Capital as lead left book-runner, with Stonebridge Greer & Co. serving as co-book-runner. Our independent auditor is Archer Pryce LLP (engagement partner: Robert C. Fenn), and the underwriters\' counsel is Whitaker & Simms LLP. This memo is intended for internal Board use and for distribution to outside counsel only; it should not be shared with the underwriting syndicate or their counsel without prior authorization from the undersigned and the General Counsel.

**[Section 1: Executive Summary --- Financial Highlights]{.underline}**

Crestline concluded fiscal year 2024 with strong financial performance across all key metrics, reinforcing our belief that the Company is well positioned to execute a successful initial public offering. The following summarizes our headline financial results for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2024:

**Revenue.** Total revenue for FY 2024 was \$187.3 million, representing year-over-year growth of 45.3% compared to FY 2023 revenue of \$128.9 million. This growth was driven by both new customer acquisition and robust expansion within our existing customer base. Subscription revenue accounted for \$168.6 million, or 90.0% of total revenue, while professional services revenue contributed \$18.7 million, or 10.0% of total revenue. Professional services remain a critical component of our land-and-expand strategy, supporting initial platform deployments and driving subsequent subscription upsells.

**Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR).** As of December 31, 2024, ARR stood at \$211.6 million, reflecting the strong trajectory of our subscription business and providing meaningful visibility into FY 2025 revenue.

**Net Loss.** GAAP net loss for FY 2024 was (\$24.8 million), or (\$0.48) per share on a basic and diluted basis, calculated on 52,000,000 weighted average shares of common stock outstanding. This represents an improvement from the FY 2023 net loss of (\$27.4 million).

**Adjusted EBITDA.** The Company generated Adjusted EBITDA of \$22.8 million in FY 2024, a significant improvement over FY 2023 performance and a clear indicator of operating leverage as we scale. A detailed reconciliation of Adjusted EBITDA to GAAP net loss is set forth in Section 5 of this memorandum.

**Dollar-Based Net Retention Rate.** Our dollar-based net retention rate was 132% for FY 2024, evidencing strong upsell and cross-sell dynamics within our customer base and reflecting the embedded expansion opportunity across our SupplyMind and Crestline Nexus platforms.

**Customer Metrics.** As of December 31, 2024, the Company served 487 customers, with 34 customers contributing ARR in excess of \$1.0 million. Total employees as of year-end were 1,247.

**Gross Margin.** Overall gross margin for FY 2024 was 70.0%. Subscription gross margin was 77.8%, reflecting the inherent scalability of our cloud-native platform. Professional services revenue supports our land-and-expand strategy and is expected to remain a modest but strategically important component of total revenue over the medium term.

In summary, the Company is well-positioned for a public offering, with strong top-line momentum, improving unit economics, and a clear path to profitability as demonstrated by positive Adjusted EBITDA. Our financial profile compares favorably to recent enterprise SaaS IPOs, and we believe the combination of rapid revenue growth, high net retention, and increasing operating leverage will resonate strongly with public market investors.

**[Section 2: Pro Forma Capitalization Summary]{.underline}**

**[2.1 Pre-IPO Capitalization (Historical --- As of December 31, 2024)]{.underline}**

As of December 31, 2024, the Company\'s capitalization consisted of the following:

**Common Stock.** There were 52,000,000 shares of common stock outstanding, \$0.0001 par value per share.

**Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock.** There were 35,416,667 shares of redeemable convertible preferred stock outstanding across four series, with an aggregate original issue price (and liquidation preference) of \$385.0 million, as follows:

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Series**   **Date Issued**   **Shares**       **Aggregate Investment**   **Price Per Share**   **Lead Investor(s)**
  ------------ ----------------- ---------------- -------------------------- --------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------
  Series A     October 2018      8,000,000        \$12.0M                    \$1.50                Graymont Ventures

  Series B     April 2020        9,500,000        \$38.0M                    \$4.00                Graymont Ventures (lead); Aldersgate Capital (participating)

  Series C     November 2021     7,916,667        \$95.0M                    \$12.00               Ridgeline Growth Partners (lead)

  Series D     June 2023         10,000,000       \$240.0M                   \$24.00               Graymont Ventures and Ridgeline Growth Partners (co-lead)

  **Total**                      **35,416,667**   **\$385.0M**                                     
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Outstanding Equity Awards.** As of December 31, 2024, there were 14,750,000 stock options outstanding under the 2024 Equity Incentive Plan with a weighted average exercise price of \$8.42 per share, and 3,200,000 restricted stock units (\"RSUs\") outstanding and unvested. The 2024 Equity Incentive Plan authorizes 18,500,000 shares, of which 17,950,000 have been issued or are outstanding and 550,000 shares remain available for future grants. In addition, the Board has approved a 2024 Employee Stock Purchase Plan (\"ESPP\") reserving 2,500,000 shares, which will become effective upon the completion of the IPO.

**Fully Diluted Share Count.** On a fully diluted basis, the Company had 105,366,667 shares outstanding as of December 31, 2024, calculated as follows: 52,000,000 shares of common stock + 35,416,667 shares issuable upon conversion of preferred stock + 14,750,000 shares subject to outstanding options + 3,200,000 shares subject to outstanding RSUs = 105,366,667.

**Total Equity.** As of December 31, 2024, total equity --- comprising common stock, preferred stock, and additional paid-in capital --- was approximately \$97.0 million. This figure reflects the aggregate of all equity-classified instruments on the Company\'s balance sheet and provides a baseline for assessing the impact of the proposed IPO on the Company\'s capitalization.

**[2.2 Pro Forma Capitalization (Giving Effect to IPO)]{.underline}**

Upon completion of the IPO, the Company\'s capitalization will be transformed in several material respects:

**Preferred Stock Conversion.** All 35,416,667 shares of redeemable convertible preferred stock will automatically convert into shares of common stock on a one-for-one basis upon the closing of the IPO. Upon conversion, the \$385.0 million aggregate liquidation preference will reclassify from mezzanine equity to permanent stockholders\' equity, and no shares of preferred stock will remain outstanding.

**Dual-Class Structure.** In connection with the IPO, the Company intends to adopt a dual-class common stock structure consisting of Class A common stock (one vote per share) and Class B common stock (ten votes per share). Class A shares will be offered to public investors. Class B shares will be held exclusively by the Company\'s co-founders:

> • Arun Ramaswamy (CEO): 12,200,000 shares of Class B common stock
>
> • Katerina \"Kate\" Litvanova (CTO): 8,600,000 shares of Class B common stock
>
> • **Total Class B:** 20,800,000 shares

**New Shares Offered.** The Company proposes to issue and sell 15,000,000 shares of Class A common stock in the IPO (all primary; no secondary shares). The underwriters will be granted an over-allotment option to purchase up to an additional 2,250,000 shares of Class A common stock (15% of the base offering).

**Post-IPO Shares Outstanding (Base Offering, No Over-Allotment).** Following the offering, total shares outstanding will be 102,416,667, comprised of:

> • Class A common stock: 81,616,667 shares
>
> • Class B common stock: 20,800,000 shares

**Founders\' Voting Power.** By virtue of the ten-to-one voting ratio of Class B shares, the co-founders will collectively control approximately 71.8% of the total voting power of the Company\'s outstanding capital stock immediately following the IPO, calculated as follows: (20,800,000 × 10) / ((81,616,667 × 1) + (20,800,000 × 10)) = 208,000,000 / 289,616,667 = 71.8%.

**[Section 3: Proposed Offering Economics and Use of Proceeds]{.underline}**

**[3.1 Offering Economics]{.underline}**

Based on preliminary discussions with Pinnacle Ridge Capital and Stonebridge Greer & Co., the Company expects the following offering economics:

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Item**                                                    **Amount**
  ----------------------------------------------------------- -------------------------------------------
  Expected IPO price range                                    \$22.00 -- \$26.00 per share

  Midpoint price                                              \$24.00 per share

  Shares offered (base)                                       15,000,000 shares of Class A common stock

  Gross proceeds at midpoint                                  \$360,000,000

  Underwriting discount (6.5%)                                \$23,400,000

  Net proceeds before expenses                                \$336,600,000

  Estimated offering expenses (excl. underwriting discount)   \$4,800,000

  **Net proceeds after all costs at midpoint**                **\$331,800,000**
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Estimated offering expenses of \$4.8 million include SEC registration fees, Nasdaq Global Select Market listing fees, printing and EDGAR filing costs, legal fees for Company counsel and underwriters\' counsel, accounting and auditor fees, transfer agent fees, and miscellaneous expenses. The underwriters will also be granted an over-allotment option to purchase up to 2,250,000 additional shares of Class A common stock. If the over-allotment option is exercised in full at the midpoint price, the Company would receive additional gross proceeds of \$54.0 million and additional net proceeds of approximately \$50.5 million (after underwriting discount).

**[3.2 Proposed Use of Proceeds]{.underline}**

The Company intends to use the estimated net proceeds of \$331.8 million (at the midpoint price) as follows:

> • **Approximately 35% (\~\$116.1 million): Research and Development.** Investment in the expansion of our AI/ML engineering team and continued product development for the Crestline Nexus and SupplyMind platforms, including next-generation predictive supply chain models, real-time optimization capabilities, and new industry-specific modules.
>
> • **Approximately 25% (\~\$83.0 million): Sales and Marketing Expansion.** Acceleration of our go-to-market strategy, including expansion of direct sales capacity in North America and establishment of regional sales offices in EMEA (London, Frankfurt) and APAC (Singapore, Tokyo).
>
> • **Approximately 15% (\~\$49.8 million): Debt Repayment.** Potential repayment or reduction of the Company\'s \$40.0 million term loan with Kestridge West Credit Partners (SOFR + 3.25%, maturing December 2027), subject to final Board determination. The remaining approximately \$9.8 million in this allocation would be applied to general corporate purposes.
>
> • **Approximately 10% (\~\$33.2 million): Acquisitions and Strategic Investments.** Opportunistic acquisitions of complementary technology, data assets, or engineering teams, and strategic investments in partnership ecosystems.
>
> • **Remainder (\~\$49.7 million): General Corporate Purposes and Working Capital.** Funding of day-to-day operations, capital expenditures, and other general corporate needs.

We note that the Kestridge West Credit Partners term loan agreement requires the Company to provide at least 30 days\' prior written notice before any public offering of the Company\'s equity securities. We must provide Kestridge West Credit Partners with written notice at least 30 days prior to pricing. We do not believe the IPO triggers the change-of-control provision under the term loan, as existing stockholders will retain approximately 71.8% of voting power post-offering.

**[3.3 Post-Offering Balance Sheet Strength]{.underline}**

Following the completion of the IPO at the midpoint price and assuming no exercise of the over-allotment option, the Company\'s pro forma cash and investment position would be approximately \$468.8 million, calculated as follows: \$94.7 million (cash and cash equivalents) + \$42.3 million (short-term investments) + \$331.8 million (estimated net proceeds) = \$468.8 million. With the \$40.0 million term loan remaining outstanding, the Company\'s net cash position would be approximately \$428.8 million, providing substantial financial flexibility to execute on our growth plan.

**[Section 4: Key Operating Metrics and Customer Analysis]{.underline}**

**[4.1 Revenue and ARR Trajectory]{.underline}**

The Company\'s revenue and ARR growth have accelerated meaningfully over the past two fiscal years:

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Metric**                      **FY 2023**       **FY 2024**       **YoY Growth**
  ------------------------------- ----------------- ----------------- -----------------
  Total Revenue                   \$128.9M          \$187.3M          45.3%

  Subscription Revenue            \$113.4M          \$168.6M          48.7%

  Professional Services Revenue   \$15.5M           \$18.7M           20.6%

  ARR (as of December 31)         \$148.2M          \$211.6M          42.8%
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ARR growth was driven by a combination of new customer acquisition (147 net new customers in FY 2024) and meaningful expansion within existing accounts. The Company\'s dollar-based net retention rate of 132% indicates that our existing customer base is growing organically at a rate well in excess of any churn, driven by strong upsell and cross-sell dynamics as customers adopt additional SupplyMind modules and expand deployments across business units and geographies.

**[4.2 Customer Metrics]{.underline}**

As of December 31, 2024, Crestline served 487 total customers, up from approximately 340 at the end of FY 2023. Of these, 34 customers contribute ARR in excess of \$1.0 million, and our average annual contract value across the customer base has continued to increase as platform adoption deepens.

**Customer Concentration.** Our largest customer, Harmon Industries Inc., represented approximately 8.2% of FY 2024 revenue (\$15.4 million). The top 10 customers collectively represented 31.7% of FY 2024 revenue (\$59.4 million). No customer other than Harmon Industries exceeded 5.0% of total revenue.

**Representative Customers.** The Company serves customers across a broad range of industry verticals, including manufacturing, retail, logistics, and consumer packaged goods. Notable new and expanding logos in FY 2024 include Harmon Industries Inc., Vantara Logistics, Inc., and several other Fortune 1000 accounts. Average contract values have trended upward as enterprise-tier deployments of SupplyMind become the primary growth driver. The Company\'s traction across multiple verticals --- and particularly in logistics and manufacturing, where supply chain complexity creates the greatest demand for AI-driven optimization --- validates the breadth of our platform\'s applicability and the sustainability of our growth trajectory.

**Industry Diversification.** Revenue is well diversified across verticals, with manufacturing (38%), retail (24%), logistics (19%), and consumer packaged goods (12%) representing the primary segments, and the remaining 7% distributed across healthcare, financial services, and other industries.

**[4.3 Competitive Positioning]{.underline}**

The Company operates in the rapidly growing market for AI-driven supply chain optimization software. Our differentiation stems from the proprietary SupplyMind AI engine, which combines machine learning, advanced statistical modeling, and real-time data integration to deliver predictive and prescriptive supply chain intelligence. The SupplyMind platform is protected by a robust intellectual property portfolio consisting of 47 issued U.S. patents, 12 pending U.S. patent applications, and 8 issued foreign patents covering core algorithmic innovations and data processing methods.

In August 2024, the Company achieved SOC 2 Type II certification, which has strengthened our competitive position in enterprise sales cycles where information security and data governance are threshold requirements. We continue to invest in security and compliance infrastructure to support the expanding regulatory requirements of our global customer base.

**[Section 5: Non-GAAP Financial Measures --- Adjusted EBITDA Reconciliation]{.underline}**

**[5.1 Rationale for Non-GAAP Presentation]{.underline}**

Management uses Adjusted EBITDA as a supplemental measure of operating performance to evaluate the Company\'s core business results, plan and forecast future periods, and benchmark against comparable public and private SaaS companies. Adjusted EBITDA is a non-GAAP financial measure and should not be considered in isolation or as a substitute for GAAP net loss or any other GAAP financial measure. We intend to present Adjusted EBITDA in the Form S-1 in compliance with Regulation G and Item 10(e) of Regulation S-K, including a reconciliation to the most directly comparable GAAP measure and appropriate cautionary language regarding limitations of non-GAAP measures. Investors should be aware that our definition of Adjusted EBITDA may not be comparable to similarly titled measures used by other companies.

**[5.2 Reconciliation of GAAP Net Loss to Adjusted EBITDA]{.underline}**

The following table reconciles GAAP net loss to Adjusted EBITDA for the fiscal years ended December 31, 2024 and December 31, 2023:

  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                       **FY 2024**             **FY 2023**
  ------------------------------------ ----------------------- -----------------------
  Net loss (GAAP)                      (\$24.8M)               (\$27.4M)

  Add: Depreciation and amortization   \$8.7M                  \$6.9M

  Add: Stock-based compensation        \$38.2M                 \$29.1M

  Add: Interest expense                \$3.1M                  \$2.8M

  Less: Interest income                (\$6.2M)                (\$4.6M)

  Add: Income tax provision            \$0.3M                  \$0.2M

  Add: Restructuring charges           \$1.2M                  \$0.6M

  **Adjusted EBITDA**                  **\$22.8M**             **\$7.6M**
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**[5.3 Commentary]{.underline}**

Adjusted EBITDA improved from \$7.6 million in FY 2023 to \$22.8 million in FY 2024, reflecting the significant operating leverage inherent in our business model as subscription revenue scales. The year-over-year improvement was driven by the 45.3% increase in revenue, combined with disciplined operating expense management, particularly in general and administrative costs.

The largest non-cash add-back in the reconciliation is stock-based compensation expense, which totaled \$38.2 million in FY 2024 compared to \$29.1 million in FY 2023. Management expects stock-based compensation to remain elevated post-IPO due to equity refresh grants, new hire awards, and the activation of the ESPP. While stock-based compensation is a meaningful real cost to stockholders through dilution, we exclude it from Adjusted EBITDA because it is a non-cash charge and because its period-to-period variability can obscure underlying operational trends.

The restructuring charges of \$1.2 million in FY 2024 relate to a workforce reduction of approximately 30 positions in our professional services organization in the second quarter of 2024, as the Company transitioned certain implementation activities to certified partners.

The achievement of \$22.8 million in Adjusted EBITDA represents significant progress toward sustainable profitability and is a meaningful milestone in the Company\'s financial trajectory. We expect Adjusted EBITDA to continue to improve as we benefit from the operating leverage of our subscription model and grow into our existing cost structure.

**[Section 6: Balance Sheet Summary and Liquidity]{.underline}**

**[6.1 Balance Sheet as of December 31, 2024 (Condensed)]{.underline}**

The following is a condensed summary of the Company\'s balance sheet as of December 31, 2024, derived from the audited financial statements prepared by Archer Pryce LLP:

**Assets**

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------

  ------------------------------------------- -----------------------------------

  Property and equipment, net                 \$18.6M

  *of which: Accumulated deficit*                              *(\$312.6M)*
