# PROSPECTUS

## CRESTLINE SOFTWARE, INC.

[●] shares of Class A common stock

This is the initial public offering of shares of Class A common stock of Crestline Software, Inc. We are offering [●] shares of Class A common stock. Prior to this offering, there has been no public market for our common stock. It is currently estimated that the initial public offering price per share will be between $22.00 and $26.00. We intend to list our Class A common stock on the Nasdaq Global Select Market under the ticker symbol "CRST."

We have two classes of common stock: Class A common stock and Class B common stock. The rights of the holders of Class A common stock and Class B common stock are identical, except with respect to voting and conversion. Each share of Class A common stock is entitled to one vote per share. Each share of Class B common stock is entitled to ten votes per share and is convertible at any time into one share of Class A common stock. Shares of Class B common stock will be held exclusively by our co-founders, Arun Ramaswamy and Katerina Litvak, and will automatically convert into Class A common stock upon transfer to a non-permitted transferee or upon the death or incapacity of the holder. # PROSPECTUS SUMMARY

This summary highlights selected information contained elsewhere in this prospectus and does not contain all of the information you should consider before investing in our Class A common stock. You should read this entire prospectus carefully, including the information set forth under "Risk Factors" and our consolidated financial statements and the related notes included elsewhere in this prospectus, before making an investment decision. Unless the context otherwise requires, references in this prospectus to "Crestline," the "Company," "we," "us," and "our" refer to Crestline Software, Inc. and its consolidated subsidiary.

## Our Company

We are an enterprise software-as-a-service ("SaaS") company providing an AI-driven supply chain optimization platform to Fortune 1000 manufacturers and retailers. Our platform, Crestline Nexus, is powered by our proprietary SupplyMind AI engine, which combines machine learning, advanced statistical modeling, and real-time data integration to deliver predictive and prescriptive supply chain intelligence, including demand forecasting, inventory optimization, and logistics routing.

We were incorporated in the State of Delaware on March 14, 2017. Our principal executive offices are located at 4200 Innovation Drive, Suite 800, Austin, Texas 78759. We operate through our wholly-owned subsidiary, Crestline Software UK Ltd., incorporated in England and Wales, which supports our European operations, and we maintain offices in San Francisco, California; London, United Kingdom; and Bangalore, India. As of December 31, 2024, we had 1,247 employees.

## Our Market Opportunity

We estimate that the total addressable market for AI-driven supply chain software will be approximately $28 billion by 2028, with a serviceable addressable market of approximately $9 billion focused on Fortune 1000 manufacturers and retailers. We believe current penetration of our serviceable market is less than 3%, providing significant runway for growth.

## Our Growth and Financial Highlights

We have experienced rapid growth. Our total revenue increased 45.3% from $128.9 million in fiscal year 2023 to $187.3 million in fiscal year 2024. Our annual recurring revenue ("ARR") was $211.6 million as of December 31, 2024, compared to $148.2 million as of December 31, 2023, representing year-over-year growth of 42.8%. Our dollar-based net retention rate was 132% for fiscal year 2024. Subscription revenue accounted for 90.0% of total revenue in fiscal year 2024.

We have a history of net losses. We incurred net losses of $24.8 million and $27.4 million for the fiscal years ended December 31, 2024 and 2023, respectively. As of December 31, 2024, we had an accumulated deficit of $312.6 million.

(1) Excludes 2,250,000 shares subject to the underwriters' over-allotment option.

## Dual-Class Common Stock Structure

In connection with this offering, our amended and restated certificate of incorporation will become effective and will provide for a dual-class common stock structure consisting of Class A common stock and Class B common stock. Each share of Class A common stock will be entitled to one vote per share, and each share of Class B common stock will be entitled to ten votes per share. Class B common stock will be held exclusively by our co-founders, Arun Ramaswamy and Katerina Litvak. Upon the closing of this offering, our co-founders will collectively hold 20,800,000 shares of Class B common stock, representing approximately 71.8% of the total voting power of our outstanding capital stock. As a result, we will be a "controlled company" under the corporate governance rules of the Nasdaq Stock Market. See "Risk Factors—Risks Related to Our Dual-Class Structure and Status as a Controlled Company" and "Description of Capital Stock."

## Emerging Growth Company Status

We are an "emerging growth company" as defined in the Jumpstart Our Business Startups Act of 2012, as amended (the "JOBS Act"). We may take advantage of certain reduced reporting and disclosure requirements that are not applicable to other public companies. See "Risk Factors—Risks Related to Our Status as an Emerging Growth Company."

## Implications of Being a Controlled Company

Because our co-founders will collectively hold more than 50% of the voting power of our outstanding capital stock following this offering, we will be a "controlled company" under the corporate governance rules of the Nasdaq Stock Market. As a controlled company, we are permitted to elect to rely on exemptions from certain Nasdaq corporate governance requirements, including the requirement that a majority of our board of directors consist of independent directors, the requirement that our compensation committee be comprised entirely of independent directors, and the requirement that director nominations be made, or recommended for selection, by a nominating committee comprised entirely of independent directors. We [expect to rely / have not yet determined whether to rely] on one or more of these exemptions. [Drafting note: The Board has not yet adopted a formal resolution regarding reliance on the controlled company exemption. See Issues Memorandum.] See "Risk Factors" and "Management—Corporate Governance."

## Corporate Information

We were incorporated in Delaware on March 14, 2017. Our principal executive offices are located at 4200 Innovation Drive, Suite 800, Austin, Texas 78759. Our telephone number is (512) 555-0142. Our website is www.crestlinesoftware.com. Information contained on, or accessible through, our website is not a part of this prospectus.

\newpage

# RISK FACTORS

An investment in our Class A common stock involves a high degree of risk. Before deciding whether to invest in our Class A common stock, you should carefully consider the specific risks set forth below, together with all of the other information contained in this prospectus, including our consolidated financial statements and the related notes. The occurrence of any of the following risks could materially and adversely affect our business, financial condition, results of operations, and prospects, and the trading price of our Class A common stock could decline, and you could lose all or part of your investment.

## Risks Related to Our Business and Industry

**We have a history of net losses, and we may not achieve or sustain profitability.**

We have incurred net losses since inception. We incurred net losses of $24.8 million and $27.4 million for the fiscal years ended December 31, 2024 and 2023, respectively. As of December 31, 2024, we had an accumulated deficit of $312.6 million and a total stockholders' deficit of $288.0 million. We expect to continue to incur operating losses and negative operating cash flows for the foreseeable future as we invest in research and development, sales and marketing, and the expansion of our operations. We may not achieve or sustain profitability, and even if we do, we may not be able to maintain or increase profitability on a quarterly or annual basis.

**We have a significant amount of indebtedness outstanding, which could adversely affect our financial condition and our ability to operate our business.**

As of December 31, 2024, we had $40.0 million of outstanding indebtedness under our senior secured term loan facility with Kestridge West Credit Partners, which matures in December 2027 and bears interest at a rate of SOFR plus 3.25%. Our obligations under the term loan are secured by a first-priority lien on substantially all of our assets, including our intellectual property (including our 47 issued U.S. patents, 12 pending U.S. patent applications, 8 issued foreign patents, and our registered trademarks "Crestline," "Crestline Nexus," and "SupplyMind") and 65% of the voting equity of our foreign subsidiary, Crestline Software UK Ltd. Our UK subsidiary has provided a limited guarantee of our obligations, capped at £5.0 million and secured by a first-priority lien on its assets. The encumbrance of our intellectual property under the term loan could limit our ability to dispose of or otherwise monetize such assets and could result in the loss of such assets in the event of a default.

**The term loan agreement contains restrictive covenants and a 30-day pre-offering notice requirement that could limit our flexibility.**

Our term loan agreement requires us to maintain minimum liquidity of $30.0 million and an ARR-based leverage ratio (total funded debt divided by ARR) of not more than 2.0x. As of December 31, 2024, our liquidity was $137.0 million and our leverage ratio was 0.19x. The agreement also restricts our ability to incur additional indebtedness, grant liens, make investments, pay dividends or repurchase stock, enter into mergers or acquisitions, and engage in affiliate transactions (in each case subject to specified thresholds and exceptions). In addition, the term loan agreement requires us to provide the lender with written notice not less than 30 days prior to the consummation of any initial public offering of our equity securities, including the anticipated date, gross proceeds, use of proceeds, and a pro forma capitalization table. Failure to provide this notice on a timely basis would constitute an event of default. We must carefully manage our offering timeline to ensure compliance with this notice requirement.

**A change of control, including certain changes in our management, could trigger an event of default under our term loan.**

The term loan agreement contains a change-of-control provision. A change of control would occur if our existing stockholders as of the closing date of the credit agreement cease to hold at least 50% of the aggregate voting power of our outstanding capital stock, including following an initial public offering. Based on our dual-class structure, our co-founders will retain approximately 71.8% of the voting power following this offering, and we do not believe this offering will trigger a change of control. However, the term loan also provides that a change of control occurs if Arun Ramaswamy ceases to serve as our Chief Executive Officer, unless a successor reasonably acceptable to the lender is appointed within 90 days. The loss of Mr. Ramaswamy as CEO could therefore trigger an event of default and acceleration of our term loan obligations.

**An adverse judgment in the Optivance patent litigation could trigger an event of default under our term loan and could have a material adverse effect on our business.**

We are currently a defendant in *Optivance, Inc. v. Crestline Software, Inc.*, Case No. 6:24-cv-00831, filed September 12, 2024 in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Texas, alleging that certain features of our SupplyMind platform infringe U.S. Patent No. 11,284,107. Our outside patent counsel estimates our potential exposure, in the event of an adverse judgment, to be between $5.0 million and $20.0 million, although we believe an unfavorable outcome is of low probability. Under our term loan agreement, one or more judgments for the payment of money in excess of $5.0 million in the aggregate that remain undischarged for 60 days constitute an event of default. An adverse judgment at the upper end of the estimated range could therefore result in acceleration of our term loan obligations if not bonded, appealed, or otherwise stayed.

**We depend on a limited number of customers for a significant portion of our revenue, and the loss of, or a material reduction in revenue from, one or more of these customers could adversely affect our results of operations.**

No single customer accounted for more than 10% of our total revenue during fiscal year 2024 or 2023. However, our largest customer, Harmon Industries Inc., represented approximately 8.2% of our total revenue in fiscal year 2024, and our top 10 customers collectively represented 31.7% of our total revenue in fiscal year 2024. The loss of, or a material reduction in revenue from, Harmon Industries or any of our other significant customers could materially and adversely affect our revenue and results of operations.

**We are subject to risks associated with pending and future litigation.**

We are a defendant in the Optivance patent infringement litigation described above. Litigation is inherently uncertain, and we may incur significant costs in defending against this and other claims. An adverse outcome in the Optivance matter or other litigation could require us to pay substantial damages, incur significant royalty or licensing obligations, redesign our products, or cease offering certain features, any of which could materially and adversely affect our business, financial condition, and results of operations.

**If we fail to maintain the security of our platform or experience a data breach, our reputation and business could be harmed.**

Our platform processes sensitive customer supply chain data. Although we achieved SOC 2 Type II certification in August 2024, a security breach, cyberattack, or data privacy incident could harm our reputation, result in customer loss, and lead to regulatory action and liability under the General Data Protection Regulation ("GDPR"), the California Consumer Privacy Act ("CCPA"), and other applicable laws.

**We depend on our key personnel, including our co-founders, and the loss of one or more of them could harm our business.**

Our success depends substantially on the continued services and performance of our co-founders, Arun Ramaswamy, our Chief Executive Officer, and Katerina Litvak, our Chief Technology Officer, as well as our other executive officers. The loss of the services of any of these individuals could adversely affect our business. We do not maintain "key person" life insurance on any of our executives.

**Our intellectual property rights may not be adequate to protect our competitive position, and we may be subject to claims that we infringe the intellectual property rights of others.**

Our competitive position depends in part on our intellectual property portfolio, which includes 47 issued U.S. patents, 12 pending U.S. patent applications, and 8 issued foreign patents. We may be unable to obtain additional patents, and our existing patents may be challenged, invalidated, or circumvented. In addition, third parties may assert infringement claims against us, as in the Optivance litigation, which could result in significant costs and liabilities.

**We face intense competition and may lack sufficient resources to compete effectively.**

The market for supply chain software is competitive and includes large, well-capitalized incumbents and point-solution vendors. Some of our competitors have greater financial, technical, and marketing resources than we do. We may not be able to compete effectively.

**Our professional services business currently operates at a negative gross margin, which adversely affects our overall gross margin.**

For fiscal year 2024, our professional services revenue was $18.7 million and our professional services cost of revenue was $18.8 million, resulting in a professional services gross margin of approximately negative 0.5%. Although we believe professional services facilitate platform adoption and customer expansion, our current professional services gross margin is negative, and we may not achieve positive professional services gross margins in the future. Our blended gross margin of 70.0% in fiscal year 2024 reflects the drag from our professional services business.

**Our growth depends on our ability to attract and retain customers and to expand within our existing customer base.**

Our growth strategy depends on acquiring new customers and expanding usage within our existing customer base, as reflected in our dollar-based net retention rate of 132% for fiscal year 2024. If our net retention rate declines, our revenue growth could slow.

**We may not realize the expected benefits from our use of the net proceeds of this offering, and our management will have broad discretion in the use of the proceeds.**

We intend to use the net proceeds of this offering for general corporate purposes, including research and development, sales and marketing, potential strategic acquisitions, working capital, and potential repayment of indebtedness. We have not allocated the net proceeds to specific uses, and our management will have broad discretion in applying the net proceeds. See "Use of Proceeds."

## Risks Related to Our Dual-Class Structure and Status as a Controlled Company

**Our dual-class common stock structure has the effect of concentrating voting control with our co-founders, which will limit the ability of other stockholders to influence corporate matters.**

Upon the closing of this offering, our co-founders will collectively hold 20,800,000 shares of Class B common stock, representing approximately 71.8% of the total voting power of our outstanding capital stock. This concentration of voting control will limit the ability of holders of our Class A common stock to influence corporate matters, including the election of directors, amendments to our certificate of incorporation, and the approval of significant corporate transactions. Even if our co-founders' economic ownership declines over time, they will continue to control a majority of the voting power as long as they retain their Class B common stock.

**Our Class B common stock has no sunset provision, so the dual-class structure may persist indefinitely.**

Our Class B common stock will automatically convert into Class A common stock only upon transfer to a non-permitted transferee or upon the death or incapacity of the holder. There is no time-based sunset (such as a fixed period after this offering) and no ownership-percentage-based sunset. As a result, the dual-class structure and the resulting concentration of voting control with our co-founders may persist indefinitely.

**Because we will be a "controlled company" under Nasdaq rules, you will have fewer protections than stockholders of companies that are not controlled companies.**

Because our co-founders will collectively hold more than 50% of the voting power of our outstanding capital stock following this offering, we will be a "controlled company" under Nasdaq Listing Rule 5615(c). As a controlled company, we may elect to rely on exemptions from certain Nasdaq corporate governance requirements, including the requirements that (i) a majority of our board of directors consist of independent directors, (ii) our compensation committee be comprised entirely of independent directors, and (iii) director nominations be selected or recommended by a nominating committee comprised entirely of independent directors. As of the date of this prospectus, only three of our directors are independent. If we rely on these exemptions, you will not have the same protections afforded to stockholders of companies that are subject to these corporate governance requirements.

**Certain provisions of our charter and bylaws could discourage, delay, or prevent a change of control that you may consider favorable.**

Our amended and restated certificate of incorporation and bylaws, which will become effective upon the closing of this offering, contain provisions that may discourage, delay, or prevent a change of control or other transaction that stockholders may consider favorable, including: a classified (staggered) board of directors; the inability of holders of Class A common stock to act by written consent; the inability of stockholders to call special meetings; the ability of the board of directors to fill vacancies; a requirement that directors may be removed only for cause and only by the affirmative vote of the holders of at least 66 2/3% of the voting power of our outstanding capital stock; and supermajority voting requirements (66 2/3%) to amend certain provisions of our charter and bylaws.

**Our exclusive forum provisions may limit your ability to obtain a favorable judicial forum for disputes with us or our directors and officers.**

Our charter provides that, unless we consent in writing to the selection of an alternative forum, the Court of Chancery of the State of Delaware will be the sole and exclusive forum for certain state-law claims, including derivative actions and claims of breach of fiduciary duty. Our charter also provides that the federal district courts of the United States will be the sole and exclusive forum for resolving any complaint asserting a cause of action arising under the Securities Act. These provisions may limit a stockholder's ability to bring a claim in a judicial forum that the stockholder finds favorable for disputes with us or our directors and officers, which may discourage such lawsuits.

**The dual-class structure may cause our Class A common stock to be excluded from certain stock indices.**

Certain stock index providers, including S&P Dow Jones Indices and FTSE Russell, have adopted policies that may exclude companies with multi-class share structures from their indices. If we are excluded from these indices, the market price of our Class A common stock could be adversely affected.

## Risks Related to Our Intellectual Property and Litigation

**We are subject to patent infringement litigation that could materially and adversely affect our business.**

On September 12, 2024, Optivance, Inc. filed a patent infringement lawsuit against us in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Texas (Case No. 6:24-cv-00831), alleging that certain features of our SupplyMind platform infringe U.S. Patent No. 11,284,107, relating to "machine-learning-based inventory forecasting." We believe the claims are without merit and intend to vigorously defend against them. The lawsuit is in the early stages, and no trial date has been set. We have not recorded a loss contingency for this matter. An adverse outcome could result in damages of between $5.0 million and $20.0 million, injunctive relief, or royalty obligations, and could trigger an event of default under our term loan. See "Legal Proceedings."

## Risks Related to Tax Matters

**Our ability to use our net operating loss carryforwards and certain other tax attributes may be limited.**

As of December 31, 2024, we had approximately $228.0 million of federal net operating loss ("NOL") carryforwards and approximately $166.0 million of state NOL carryforwards. A substantial portion of our federal NOLs does not expire but is subject to an 80% of taxable income limitation. Our ability to use our NOL carryforwards may be limited if we experience an "ownership change" as defined in Section 382 of the Internal Revenue Code, which could occur in connection with this offering or other equity transactions. We have not yet completed a Section 382 study. If an ownership change occurs, our ability to use our NOLs to offset future taxable income could be significantly limited, which could result in increased future tax liability.

## Risks Related to Our Indebtedness

**The term loan is secured by substantially all of our assets, including our intellectual property.**

See the risk factor above regarding our indebtedness. The first-priority lien on substantially all of our assets, including our intellectual property, could limit our operational and strategic flexibility and could result in the loss of key assets in the event of a default.

## Risks Related to Our Common Stock and This Offering

**You will experience immediate and substantial dilution in the pro forma net tangible book value per share of the Class A common stock you purchase.**

The initial public offering price per share is expected to be substantially higher than the pro forma net tangible book value per share of our Class A common stock. If you purchase shares of Class A common stock in this offering, you will suffer immediate and substantial dilution of approximately $19.85 per share from the initial public offering price of $24.00 (the midpoint of the estimated range) to the pro forma net tangible book value per share of $4.15 after giving effect to this offering. See "Dilution."

**A substantial number of shares of our common stock will be eligible for sale after the lock-up period expires, which could cause the market price of our Class A common stock to decline.**

Substantially all of our outstanding common stock and shares issuable upon conversion of our preferred stock, exercise of outstanding stock options, and vesting of outstanding RSUs will be subject to 180-day lock-up agreements. After the lock-up period expires, those shares may be sold in the public market. Sales of a substantial number of shares, or the perception that such sales may occur, could cause the market price of our Class A common stock to decline.

**We do not intend to pay dividends on our Class A common stock, and our term loan agreement restricts our ability to pay dividends.**

We have never declared or paid any cash dividends on our common stock. We do not intend to pay any cash dividends in the foreseeable future. In addition, our term loan agreement prohibits us from paying dividends or making other distributions on our equity interests without the lender's consent. Any return to stockholders will therefore be in the form of stock price appreciation, which may never occur.

**Our stock-based compensation expense is significant, and we may not accurately estimate the fair value of our common stock for purposes of recording stock-based compensation.**

Our stock-based compensation expense was $38.2 million and $26.4 million for fiscal years 2024 and 2023, respectively. For periods prior to the establishment of a public trading market, the fair value of our common stock was determined by our board of directors with the assistance of independent third-party valuation specialists. Determining the fair value of our common stock involves significant judgment and estimates. If our estimates of the fair value of our common stock were inaccurate, our stock-based compensation expense may have been understated or overstated, and we may be required to record additional compensation expense. In addition, certain stock options granted in the first quarter of 2023 were priced based on a September 30, 2022 valuation of $9.50 per share, while a subsequent valuation as of March 31, 2023 determined the fair value to be $14.20 per share. This increase could indicate that the exercise price of those grants was below the fair market value of our common stock at the time of grant, which could have adverse tax consequences under Section 409A of the Internal Revenue Code and could result in additional stock-based compensation expense.

## Risks Related to Our Status as an Emerging Growth Company

**As an emerging growth company, we may take advantage of reduced disclosure requirements, which may make our securities less attractive to investors.**

We are an emerging growth company and may take advantage of exemptions from various reporting requirements, including reduced executive compensation disclosure and exemption from the requirement to obtain an auditor attestation on our internal control over financial reporting under Section 404(b) of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act. We cannot predict whether investors will find our Class A common stock less attractive as a result of our reliance on these exemptions.

# DILUTION

The pro forma net tangible book value per share of our common stock as of December 31, 2024 was approximately $1.06 per share, after giving effect to the automatic conversion of all outstanding shares of our redeemable convertible preferred stock into 35,416,667 shares of common stock upon the closing of this offering. Pro forma net tangible book value per share represents the amount of our total tangible assets less total liabilities, divided by the number of shares of common stock then outstanding.

After giving effect to the sale of 15,000,000 shares of Class A common stock in this offering at an assumed initial public offering price of $24.00 per share (the midpoint of the estimated range), after deducting the underwriting discounts and commissions and estimated offering expenses payable by us, our pro forma net tangible book value per share as of December 31, 2024 would have been approximately $4.15 per share. This represents an immediate increase in pro forma net tangible book value per share of approximately $3.09 per share to our existing stockholders, and an immediate and substantial dilution to new investors of approximately $19.85 per share.

The following table illustrates this dilution:

| | Assumed offering price per share | $24.00 |
|---|---|---|
| Pro forma net tangible book value per share before this offering | | $1.06 |
| Increase per share attributable to this offering | | $3.09 |
| Pro forma net tangible book value per share after this offering | | $4.15 |
| Dilution per share to new investors | | $19.85 |

The dilution computation is determined as follows:

| | |
|---|---|
| Pro forma net tangible book value before offering | $92.9 million |
| Plus: net proceeds from this offering | $331.8 million |
| Pro forma net tangible book value after offering | $424.7 million |
| Pro forma shares outstanding before offering | 87,416,667 |
| Plus: shares offered in this offering | 15,000,000 |
| Pro forma shares outstanding after offering | 102,416,667 |

If the underwriters exercise their over-allotment option in full, the pro forma net tangible book value per share after this offering would be approximately $4.54 per share, and the dilution to new investors would be approximately $19.46 per share. See Issues Memorandum.]

The foregoing discussion and table do not give effect to (i) the exercise of outstanding stock options with respect to 14,750,000 shares of common stock at a weighted-average exercise price of $8.42 per share, (ii) the vesting and settlement of 3,200,000 outstanding RSUs, (iii) 550,000 shares available for future grant under our 2024 Equity Incentive Plan, or (iv) 2,500,000 shares reserved under our 2024 Employee Stock Purchase Plan. The exercise of options or vesting of RSUs will result in further dilution to new investors.

# DESCRIPTION OF SECURITIES TO BE REGISTERED

The following description of our capital stock summarizes the material terms of our amended and restated certificate of incorporation and amended and restated bylaws, each of which will become effective upon the closing of this offering. For a more complete description, see the full text of these documents, which are filed as exhibits to this registration statement.

## General

Upon the closing of this offering, our amended and restated certificate of incorporation will become effective. It will authorize 1,500,000,000 shares of capital stock, consisting of (i) 1,200,000,000 shares of Class A common stock, par value $0.0001 per share, (ii) 200,000,000 shares of Class B common stock, par value $0.0001 per share, and (iii) 100,000,000 shares of preferred stock, par value $0.0001 per share. Upon the closing of this offering, all outstanding shares of our Series A, Series B, Series C, and Series D redeemable convertible preferred stock (an aggregate of 35,416,667 shares) will automatically convert into shares of Class A common stock on a one-for-one basis, and no shares of preferred stock will remain outstanding.

## Class A Common Stock

Holders of Class A common stock are entitled to one vote per share on all matters submitted to a vote of stockholders, voting together with the holders of Class B common stock as a single class, except as otherwise required by law. Holders of Class A common stock are entitled to receive dividends when, as, and if declared by our board of directors, on an equal per share basis with holders of Class B common stock. In the event of a liquidation, dissolution, or winding up, holders of Class A common stock are entitled to share ratably in the assets remaining after payment of liabilities and any amounts due to holders of preferred stock, on an equal per share basis with holders of Class B common stock. Class A common stock has no conversion, redemption, or sinking fund rights.

## Class B Common Stock

Holders of Class B common stock are entitled to ten votes per share on all matters submitted to a vote of stockholders, voting together with the holders of Class A common stock as a single class, except as otherwise required by law. Holders of Class B common stock are entitled to receive dividends on an equal per share basis with holders of Class A common stock. In the event of a liquidation, dissolution, or winding up, holders of Class B common stock are entitled to share ratably in the remaining assets on an equal per share basis with holders of Class A common stock.

Each share of Class B common stock is convertible at the option of the holder into one share of Class A common stock at any time. In addition, each share of Class B common stock will automatically convert into one share of Class A common stock upon (i) any transfer of such share to a person or entity other than a permitted transferee, or (ii) the death or incapacity of the holder, if the holder is a natural person. "Permitted transferees" include the holder's spouse, domestic partner, parents, children, grandchildren, and trusts for the benefit of such persons, and entities wholly owned by the holder or permitted transferees, as well as transfers between our two co-founders. There is no time-based or ownership-percentage-based sunset on the Class B common stock.

Class B common stock will be held exclusively by our co-founders, Arun Ramaswamy (12,200,000 shares) and Katerina Litvak (8,600,000 shares), for an aggregate of 20,800,000 shares. Upon the closing of this offering, the co-founders will collectively hold approximately 71.8% of the total voting power of our outstanding capital stock.

## Preferred Stock

Our board of directors is authorized, subject to limitations prescribed by law, to issue shares of preferred stock in one or more series and to fix the designations, powers, preferences, and rights of each series without further stockholder approval. The issuance of preferred stock could adversely affect the voting power of holders of Class A common stock and could be used as a method of discouraging, delaying, or preventing a change of control.

## Registration Rights

In June 2023, we entered into an Investor Rights Agreement with certain holders of our preferred stock. Pursuant to the Investor Rights Agreement, those holders are entitled to demand registration rights and S-3 piggyback registration rights, in each case commencing 180 days after the closing of this offering, subject to the terms and conditions of the agreement. The Investor Rights Agreement survives the closing of this offering. The Voting Agreement and Right of First Refusal and Co-Sale Agreement entered into in June 2023 will terminate upon the closing of this offering.

## Anti-Takeover Effects of Our Charter and Bylaws

Our charter and bylaws contain provisions that may discourage, delay, or prevent a change of control, including a classified board of directors, the inability of holders of Class A common stock to act by written consent, the inability of stockholders to call special meetings, the ability of the board to fill vacancies, removal of directors only for cause and only by a 66 2/3% vote, and supermajority (66 2/3%) voting requirements to amend certain provisions of our charter and bylaws.

## Exclusive Forum

Our charter provides that the Court of Chancery of the State of Delaware is the sole and exclusive forum for certain state-law claims, including derivative actions and claims of breach of fiduciary duty, and that the federal district courts of the United States are the sole and exclusive forum for resolving any complaint asserting a cause of action arising under the Securities Act.

# BUSINESS

## Overview

We are an enterprise SaaS company providing an AI-driven supply chain optimization platform to Fortune 1000 manufacturers and retailers. Our Crestline Nexus platform, powered by our proprietary SupplyMind AI engine, delivers predictive and prescriptive supply chain intelligence, including demand forecasting, inventory optimization, and logistics routing. We serve customers across manufacturing, retail, logistics, and consumer packaged goods verticals.

## Our Products

**Crestline Nexus Platform.** Our unified SaaS platform provides end-to-end supply chain optimization, with real-time data ingestion from ERP, WMS, TMS, and IoT systems, pre-built connectors for over 100 enterprise data sources, and a multi-tenant, cloud-native architecture.

**SupplyMind AI Engine.** Our proprietary machine learning models, including deep learning and reinforcement learning models trained on billions of supply chain events, power demand sensing, safety stock optimization, network design, and logistics routing. Our algorithms are protected by our patent portfolio.

## Intellectual Property

As of December 31, 2024, our intellectual property portfolio included 47 issued U.S. patents, 12 pending U.S. patent applications, and 8 issued foreign patents covering core algorithmic innovations and data processing methods. Our material trademarks include "Crestline," "Crestline Nexus," and "SupplyMind," each registered with the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office. We also rely on trade secrets and proprietary training data. We are actively defending our intellectual property, including in the Optivance litigation. See "Legal Proceedings."

## Customers

As of December 31, 2024, we served 487 customers, of which 34 contributed ARR in excess of $1.0 million. Our largest customer, Harmon Industries Inc., represented approximately 8.2% of our total revenue in fiscal year 2024, and our top 10 customers collectively represented 31.7% of our total revenue. No single customer other than Harmon Industries exceeded 5.0% of total revenue. Our revenue is diversified across manufacturing (38%), retail (24%), logistics (19%), and consumer packaged goods (12%), with the remaining 7% across healthcare, financial services, and other industries.

## Competition

The market for supply chain software is competitive and includes large, well-capitalized ERP vendors and point-solution vendors. We believe our principal competitive advantages are our AI-native architecture, our proprietary SupplyMind AI engine, our patent portfolio, our high net retention rate, and our Fortune 1000 customer base.

## Employees

As of December 31, 2024, we had 1,247 employees.

## Corporate Information and History

We were incorporated in Delaware on March 14, 2017. Our principal executive offices are located at 4200 Innovation Drive, Suite 800, Austin, Texas 78759. We operate through our wholly-owned subsidiary, Crestline Software UK Ltd., incorporated in England and Wales.

## Available Information

Our website is www.crestlinesoftware.com. Information on our website is not part of this prospectus.

# LEGAL PROCEEDINGS

On September 12, 2024, Optivance, Inc. filed a patent infringement lawsuit against us in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Texas (Case No. 6:24-cv-00831), alleging that certain features of our SupplyMind platform infringe U.S. Patent No. 11,284,107, relating to "machine-learning-based inventory forecasting." We believe the claims are without merit and intend to vigorously defend against them. The lawsuit is in the early stages of litigation, and no trial date has been set. We have not recorded a loss contingency for this matter because management believes it is not probable that a loss has been incurred. Based on the advice of outside patent counsel, we estimate the potential range of loss, in the event of an adverse judgment, to be between $5.0 million and $20.0 million, although an unfavorable outcome is considered to have a low probability. An adverse judgment could also trigger an event of default under our term loan agreement. See "Risk Factors."

From time to time, we are involved in legal proceedings, claims, and regulatory matters arising in the ordinary course of business. We do not believe that the outcome of any current matter, other than the Optivance litigation, individually or in the aggregate, will have a material adverse effect on our financial position, results of operations, or cash flows.

# MANAGEMENT'S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS

The following discussion and analysis should be read together with our consolidated financial statements and the related notes included elsewhere in this prospectus. This discussion contains forward-looking statements that involve risks and uncertainties. Our actual results may differ materially from those anticipated in these forward-looking statements. See "Special Note Regarding Forward-Looking Statements."

## Overview

We are an enterprise SaaS company providing an AI-driven supply chain optimization platform. We generate revenue from (i) subscription fees for access to our cloud-based platform, recognized ratably over the contractual term (typically one to three years), and (ii) professional services, including implementation, configuration, training, and consulting, recognized as services are delivered. Subscription revenue accounted for 90.0% of our total revenue in fiscal year 2024.

## Key Operating Metrics

We monitor the following key operating metrics:

- **ARR.** Our ARR was $211.6 million as of December 31, 2024, compared to $148.2 million as of December 31, 2023, an increase of 42.8%.
- **Dollar-based net retention rate.** Our dollar-based net retention rate was 132% for fiscal year 2024.
- **Customers.** We served 487 customers as of December 31, 2024, of which 34 contributed ARR in excess of $1.0 million.

## Results of Operations

The following table sets forth our results of operations for the fiscal years ended December 31, 2024 and 2023 (in thousands):

| | 2024 | 2023 | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Revenue | $187,300 | $128,900 | $58,400 |
| Cost of revenue | 56,200 | 41,300 | 14,900 |
| Gross profit | 131,100 | 87,600 | 43,500 |
| Operating expenses | 159,100 | 118,700 | 40,400 |
| Loss from operations | (28,000) | (31,100) | 3,100 |
| Interest income | 6,200 | 5,800 | (400) |
| Interest expense | (3,100) | (3,100) | — |
| Other income, net | 400 | 1,100 | (700) |
| Net loss before income taxes | (24,500) | (27,300) | 2,800 |
| Income tax provision | 300 | 100 | (200) |
| Net loss | $(24,800) | $(27,400) | $2,600 |

**Revenue.** Total revenue increased 45.3% from $128.9 million in fiscal year 2023 to $187.3 million in fiscal year 2024, driven by new customer acquisition and expansion within our existing customer base. Subscription revenue increased from $116.4 million to $168.6 million, and professional services revenue increased from $12.5 million to $18.7 million.

**Gross profit and gross margin.** Gross profit increased from $87.6 million (67.9% gross margin) in fiscal year 2023 to $131.1 million (70.0% gross margin) in fiscal year 2024. The increase in gross margin was primarily attributable to the increasing proportion of subscription revenue. Subscription gross margin was 77.8% in fiscal year 2024. Professional services gross margin was approximately negative 0.5% in fiscal year 2024, as professional services cost of revenue ($18.8 million) exceeded professional services revenue ($18.7 million).

**Operating expenses.** Total operating expenses increased from $118.7 million in fiscal year 2023 to $159.1 million in fiscal year 2024, driven by increased investment in research and development ($62.8 million, up from $45.2 million), sales and marketing ($71.4 million, up from $52.1 million), and general and administrative ($24.9 million, up from $21.4 million). Stock-based compensation expense was $38.2 million in fiscal year 2024, up from $26.4 million in fiscal year 2023.

**Net loss.** Net loss was $24.8 million in fiscal year 2024, compared to $27.4 million in fiscal year 2023, an improvement of $2.6 million, reflecting revenue growth and operating leverage.

## Non-GAAP Financial Measure: Adjusted EBITDA

We present Adjusted EBITDA as a supplemental non-GAAP financial measure. We define Adjusted EBITDA as net loss adjusted for depreciation and amortization, stock-based compensation, interest income, interest expense, income tax provision, and restructuring charges. Adjusted EBITDA is not a measure of financial performance under GAAP and should not be considered in isolation or as a substitute for GAAP net loss. See "Risk Factors" regarding the limitations of non-GAAP measures.

The following table reconciles GAAP net loss to Adjusted EBITDA (in thousands):

## Liquidity and Capital Resources

As of December 31, 2024, we had $94.7 million in cash and cash equivalents and $42.3 million in short-term investments, for total liquidity of $137.0 million. We have funded our operations primarily through the issuance of redeemable convertible preferred stock and, to a lesser extent, revenue from operations and borrowings under our term loan facility.

**Cash flows.** Net cash provided by operating activities was $29.4 million and $12.9 million for fiscal years 2024 and 2023, respectively. Net cash used in investing activities was $11.9 million and $211.7 million for fiscal years 2024 and 2023, respectively. Net cash used in (provided by) financing activities was $(0.8) million and $258.7 million for fiscal years 2024 and 2023, respectively.

**Term loan.** We have a senior secured term loan facility with Kestridge West Credit Partners. As of December 31, 2024, $40.0 million was outstanding under the facility, which matures in December 2027 and bears interest at SOFR plus 3.25%. The facility is secured by a first-priority lien on substantially all of our assets, including our intellectual property. The facility requires us to maintain minimum liquidity of $30.0 million and an ARR-based leverage ratio of not more than 2.0x. As of December 31, 2024, we were in compliance with all financial covenants. The facility also requires us to provide the lender with at least 30 days' prior written notice before any public offering of our equity securities. See "Risk Factors" and Note 8 to our consolidated financial statements.

**Going concern.** Management believes that our existing cash resources, together with anticipated cash from operations, will be sufficient to fund operations for at least 12 months from the date of issuance of our consolidated financial statements. We have incurred net losses since inception and had an accumulated deficit of $312.6 million as of December 31, 2024.

## Contractual Obligations

Our material contractual obligations include our term loan (with $5.0 million, $10.0 million, and $25.0 million due in 2025, 2026, and 2027, respectively), our operating lease commitments (with undiscounted future minimum lease payments of $32.6 million), and our non-cancellable hosting and infrastructure commitments (approximately $12.0 million over the next three years).

## Critical Accounting Policies and Estimates

Our critical accounting policies and estimates include revenue recognition for arrangements with multiple performance obligations (including the estimation of stand-alone selling prices), the valuation of common stock underlying stock-based compensation awards, and the recognition of stock-based compensation expense. Our independent auditor identified these matters as critical audit matters. See the Report of Independent Registered Public Accounting Firm.

## Executive Officers

**Arun Ramaswamy** has served as our Co-Founder, Chief Executive Officer, and Chairman of the Board since our incorporation in March 2017. Prior to founding Crestline, Mr. Ramaswamy served as an Engagement Manager at McKinsey & Company. He holds a B.S. in Electrical Engineering and Computer Science from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology and an M.B.A. from the Stanford Graduate School of Business.

**Katerina Litvak** has served as our Co-Founder and Chief Technology Officer since our incorporation in March 2017. Prior to co-founding Crestline, Dr. Litvak was a researcher at a major technology company's artificial intelligence research laboratory. She holds a Ph.D. in Computer Science from Carnegie Mellon University.

**Michael D. Hartwell** has served as our Chief Financial Officer since 2020. Prior to joining Crestline, Mr. Hartwell served as Vice President of Finance at a publicly traded enterprise software company. He is a Certified Public Accountant and holds an M.B.A. from the Wharton School of Business at the University of Pennsylvania.

**Sandra Y. Chen** has served as our General Counsel and Corporate Secretary since 2019. Prior to joining Crestline, Ms. Chen was an associate at Thorncroft & Wexler LLP. She holds a J.D. from Columbia Law School.

## Directors

**Priya Venkatesh** has served as a member of our board of directors since 2018. Ms. Venkatesh is the Managing Partner of Graymont Ventures, where she leads the firm's investments in enterprise software and supply chain technology. Ms. Venkatesh is not independent due to her affiliation with Graymont Ventures, a significant stockholder.

**Thomas J. Moreno** has served as a member of our board of directors since 2021. Mr. Moreno is the former Chief Executive Officer of Lantheon Corp., an enterprise SaaS company. Mr. Moreno is an independent director and serves as a member of our Audit Committee and Compensation Committee.

**Dr. Helen Park** has served as a member of our board of directors since 2022. Dr. Park is a Professor of Operations Research at Stanford University. Dr. Park is an independent director and serves as a member of our Audit Committee.

**James Kwon** has served as a member of our board of directors since 2020. Mr. Kwon is a Partner at Ridgeline Growth Partners. Mr. Kwon is not independent due to his affiliation with Ridgeline Growth Partners, a significant stockholder.

**Adaora Obi** has served as a member of our board of directors since 2023. Ms. Obi is the former General Counsel of Nexara Technologies. Ms. Obi is an independent director, serves as the Chair of our Audit Committee, and qualifies as an "audit committee financial expert" within the meaning of SEC rules.

## Equity Incentive Plans

**2024 Equity Incentive Plan.** Our 2024 Equity Incentive Plan (the "2024 Plan"), adopted by our board of directors and approved by our stockholders on January 15, 2024, authorizes the issuance of up to 18,500,000 shares of common stock pursuant to stock options, RSUs, stock appreciation rights, and other equity-based awards. The 2024 Plan is the successor to our 2017 Stock Option Plan, and all outstanding awards under the 2017 Plan were rolled into the 2024 Plan. As of December 31, 2024, 14,750,000 stock options and 3,200,000 RSUs were outstanding under the 2024 Plan, and 550,000 shares remained available for future grant. Stock options generally vest over four years (with a one-year cliff) and have a contractual term of ten years. RSUs generally vest over four years. Awards under the 2024 Plan are subject to double-trigger acceleration upon a change of control. The 2024 Plan is administered by our Compensation Committee, with Jansen Delacroix Advisory serving as independent compensation consultant.

**2024 Employee Stock Purchase Plan.** Our 2024 Employee Stock Purchase Plan (the "ESPP"), adopted and approved on January 15, 2024, reserves 2,500,000 shares of Class A common stock and is intended to qualify under Section 423 of the Internal Revenue Code. The ESPP will become effective upon the closing of this offering. The ESPP provides for a 15% discount and a look-back feature, with 24-month offering periods, and includes an annual evergreen increase of the lesser of 1% of outstanding shares or 2,000,000 shares.

# CERTAIN RELATIONSHIPS AND RELATED PARTY TRANSACTIONS

## Vantara Logistics, Inc. SaaS License Agreement

In April 2023, we entered into a SaaS license agreement with Vantara Logistics, Inc. ("Vantara"), a portfolio company of Graymont Ventures. Priya Venkatesh, a member of our board of directors, is the Managing Partner of Graymont Ventures and also serves on the board of directors of Vantara. The agreement provides for annual subscription fees of $1.2 million over a three-year term, for a total contract value of $3.6 million. The transaction was reviewed and approved by our Audit Committee, with Ms. Venkatesh recused from the deliberation and vote. The Audit Committee concluded that the terms were comparable to those offered to similarly situated customers. Revenue recognized from Vantara was $1.2 million during fiscal year 2024 and $0.9 million during fiscal year 2023. Accounts receivable from Vantara was $0.3 million and $0.25 million as of December 31, 2024 and 2023, respectively.

[Drafting note: This related party transaction was disclosed by Ms. Venkatesh in her director questionnaire but was not identified as a related party transaction by our Chief Financial Officer in his questionnaire, and was presented as an ordinary customer (including as a customer case study) in the CFO's IPO readiness memorandum and the roadshow presentation. In addition, the $1.2 million annual contract value exceeds the $500,000 affiliate-transaction notification threshold under our term loan agreement, and notification to the lender may be required. See Issues Memorandum.]

## Preferred Stock Financings

Graymont Ventures participated in our Series A (October 2018), Series B (April 2020), and Series D (June 2023) financing rounds, and Ridgeline Growth Partners participated in our Series C (November 2021) and Series D (June 2023) financing rounds. Aldersgate Capital participated in our Series B financing. These financings were conducted on terms negotiated at arm's length and approved by our board of directors.

## Investor Rights Agreement and Voting Agreement

In June 2023, we entered into an Investor Rights Agreement, a Voting Agreement, and a Right of First Refusal and Co-Sale Agreement with certain of our preferred stockholders, including Graymont Ventures and Ridgeline Growth Partners. The Voting Agreement and Right of First Refusal and Co-Sale Agreement will terminate upon the closing of this offering. The Investor Rights Agreement provides registration rights that survive this offering.

## Director and Officer Indemnification

Our charter and bylaws provide for the indemnification of our directors and officers to the fullest extent permitted by Delaware law. We intend to maintain directors' and officers' liability insurance with appropriate public company coverage limits.

## Related Party Policies

We have an Audit Committee that reviews and approves related party transactions. We intend to adopt a formal related party transaction approval policy in connection with this offering.

| Cash and cash equivalents, beginning of year | 78,400 | 8,800 |
| **Cash and cash equivalents, end of year** | **$94,700** | **$68,500** |

Supplemental disclosure: Cash paid for interest was $2.9 million and $2.9 million for fiscal years 2024 and 2023, respectively. Cash paid for income taxes was $0.3 million and $0.1 million for fiscal years 2024 and 2023, respectively.

[Drafting note: The consolidated statements of cash flows reflect $15.0 million of term loan borrowings in fiscal year 2023 and $5.0 million of term loan repayments in fiscal year 2024, while the balance sheet reflects a constant $40.0 million term loan balance at both year-ends. These amounts do not reconcile. In addition, the term loan summary states that the credit agreement was dated December 15, 2022, with $40.0 million drawn at closing, while Note 8 to the financial statements states that the facility was entered into in June 2023. These inconsistencies must be resolved. See Issues Memorandum.]

## Notes to Consolidated Financial Statements (Selected)

**Note 1—Organization and Description of Business.** Crestline Software, Inc. was incorporated in Delaware on March 14, 2017, and is headquartered in Austin, Texas. The Company is an enterprise SaaS company providing an AI-driven supply chain optimization platform. The Company operates through its wholly-owned subsidiary, Crestline Software UK Ltd. As of December 31, 2024, the Company had 1,247 employees. The Company has incurred net losses since inception and had an accumulated deficit of $312.6 million as of December 31, 2024. As of December 31, 2024, the Company had cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments of $137.0 million. Management believes existing cash resources, together with anticipated cash from operations, will be sufficient to fund operations for at least 12 months.

**Note 2—Summary of Significant Accounting Policies.** The consolidated financial statements are prepared in conformity with GAAP and include the accounts of Crestline Software, Inc. and its wholly-owned subsidiary. Revenue is recognized in accordance with ASC 606. Subscription revenue is recognized ratably over the contractual term (typically one to three years). Professional services revenue is recognized as services are delivered. For arrangements with multiple performance obligations, the transaction price is allocated based on relative stand-alone selling prices. The Company operates as a single operating segment.

**Note 3—Revenue.** Revenue disaggregated by type: subscription $168.6 million and $116.4 million; professional services $18.7 million and $12.5 million, for fiscal years 2024 and 2023, respectively. Revenue disaggregated by geography (based on customer billing address): United States $146.1 million and $100.5 million; Europe $26.2 million and $18.0 million; Rest of World $15.0 million and $10.3 million, for fiscal years 2024 and 2023, respectively. Deferred revenue was $48.3 million and $33.6 million as of December 31, 2024 and 2023, respectively. Remaining performance obligations were approximately $285.0 million as of December 31, 2024, of which approximately 62% is expected to be recognized over the next 12 months. No single customer accounted for more than 10% of total revenue; one customer represented approximately 8.2% of total revenue in fiscal year 2024.

**Note 8—Debt.** The Company has a senior secured term loan facility with Kestridge West Credit Partners providing for borrowings of up to $50.0 million. The facility matures in December 2027 and bears interest at SOFR plus 3.25%. As of December 31, 2024 and 2023, the outstanding principal balance was $40.0 million. The credit agreement contains financial covenants, including a minimum liquidity requirement of $30.0 million and an annual recurring revenue-based leverage ratio not to exceed 2.0x. As of December 31, 2024, the Company was in compliance with all financial covenants. The term loan includes customary events of default, a change-of-control provision, and a requirement that the Company provide the lender with at least 30 days' prior written notice before any public offering. [Drafting note: Note 8 of the audited financial statements states that the facility was entered into in June 2023, while the term loan summary states the credit agreement is dated December 15, 2022. See Issues Memorandum.]

**Note 10—Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock.** The Company has issued four series of redeemable convertible preferred stock: Series A (8,000,000 shares, $1.50 per share, $12.0 million aggregate); Series B (9,500,000 shares, $4.00 per share, $38.0 million aggregate); Series C (7,916,667 shares, $12.00 per share, $95.0 million aggregate); and Series D (10,000,000 shares, $24.00 per share, $240.0 million aggregate), for a total of 35,416,667 shares and $385.0 million aggregate liquidation preference. All outstanding shares will automatically convert into common stock upon a Qualified IPO (a firm commitment underwritten IPO with aggregate gross proceeds of at least $100.0 million). The preferred stock is classified in mezzanine equity. Beginning in June 2028, upon the election of holders of at least 66 2/3% of the outstanding preferred stock, the Company may be required to redeem all outstanding shares at the original issue price plus declared but unpaid dividends, payable in three equal annual installments.

**Note 12—Stock-Based Compensation.** The 2024 Equity Incentive Plan authorizes 18,500,000 shares. As of December 31, 2024, 14,750,000 stock options and 3,200,000 RSUs were outstanding, and 550,000 shares remained available for future grant. Stock-based compensation expense was $38.2 million and $26.4 million for fiscal years 2024 and 2023, respectively. As of December 31, 2024, total unrecognized stock-based compensation expense was approximately $62.5 million, expected to be recognized over a weighted-average period of 2.8 years. Selected historical 409A valuations of the Company's common stock: June 30, 2022—$7.80; September 30, 2022—$9.50; March 31, 2023—$14.20; September 30, 2023—$16.50; March 31, 2024—$18.20; September 30, 2024—$19.60. During the three months ended March 31, 2023, the Company granted 2,400,000 stock options with an exercise price of $9.50 per share, based on the September 30, 2022 valuation; a subsequent valuation as of March 31, 2023 determined the fair value to be $14.20 per share. [Drafting note: The 409A valuation history in Note 12(e) of the audited financial statements differs materially from the 409A valuation history in the equity plan records. See Issues Memorandum.]

**Note 13—Income Taxes.** As of December 31, 2024, the Company had approximately $228.0 million of federal NOL carryforwards and approximately $166.0 million of state NOL carryforwards. The Company maintained a full valuation allowance against its net deferred tax assets. Utilization of NOL carryforwards may be subject to limitations under Section 382 of the Internal Revenue Code. The Company has not yet completed a Section 382 study.

**Note 14—Commitments and Contingencies.** The Company is a defendant in *Optivance, Inc. v. Crestline Software, Inc.* (Case No. 6:24-cv-00831, E.D. Tex.), filed September 12, 2024, alleging infringement of U.S. Patent No. 11,284,107. The Company has not recorded a loss contingency; the estimated range of loss is $5.0 million to $20.0 million, with an unfavorable outcome considered low probability. The Company has non-cancellable hosting and infrastructure commitments of approximately $12.0 million over the next three years.

**Note 15—Related Party Transactions.** In April 2023, the Company entered into a SaaS license agreement with Vantara Logistics, Inc., a portfolio company of Graymont Ventures. Priya Venkatesh, a director, is the Managing Partner of Graymont Ventures. The agreement provides for annual subscription fees of $1.2 million over a three-year term. The transaction was approved by the Audit Committee with Ms. Venkatesh recused. Revenue recognized from Vantara was $1.2 million in fiscal year 2024 and $0.9 million in fiscal year 2023.

**Note 17—Subsequent Events.** The Company evaluated subsequent events through March [●], 2025. In February 2025, the Company filed a Registration Statement on Form S-1 with the SEC in connection with the proposed IPO. Upon the closing of the IPO, all outstanding preferred stock will convert into common stock, the certificate of incorporation will be amended and restated to establish the dual-class structure, and the ESPP will become effective. [Drafting note: The subsequent events note states that the Form S-1 was filed in February 2025, while the CFO memorandum and underwriting term sheet target a March 2025 filing. See Issues Memorandum.]

{"content": "# ISSUES MEMORANDUM

**TO:** Sarah K. Norden, Esq. and James T. Okafor, Esq., Thorncroft & Wexler LLP; Michael D. Hartwell, Chief Financial Officer; Sandra Y. Chen, General Counsel
**FROM:** [Drafting Counsel]
**DATE:** [●], 2025
**RE:** Inconsistencies, Disclosure Gaps, and Risks Identified in Connection with the Form S-1 Registration Statement for the Proposed Initial Public Offering of Crestline Software, Inc.
**PRIVILEGED AND CONFIDENTIAL — PREPARED AT THE DIRECTION OF COUNSEL**

## I. Introduction and Scope

This memorandum identifies inconsistencies, disclosure gaps, and risks identified during our review of the source documents made available in connection with the preparation of the Registration Statement on Form S-1 for the proposed initial public offering ("IPO") of Crestline Software, Inc. (the "Company"). The documents reviewed include: (1) the audited consolidated financial statements for fiscal years 2023 and 2024; (2) the CFO's IPO readiness memorandum dated February 14, 2025; (3) the amended and restated certificate of incorporation and bylaws; (4) the management (director and officer) questionnaire responses; (5) the term loan summary; (6) the underwriting term sheet and form of lock-up agreement; (7) the equity plan summary and grant records; and (8) the draft roadshow presentation.

Each item below is stated separately so that it can be tracked to resolution. Items are grouped by category for ease of reference, but each numbered item is a distinct issue. Where a figure in the Company's internal materials conflicts with the audited financial statements, the audited financial statements control for purposes of the Form S-1, and the conflict is flagged here for correction of the internal materials and for confirmation that the audited figures are correct.

This memorandum is not exhaustive of all matters that may arise during the SEC review process, and additional issues may be identified as drafting continues.

---

## II. Financial Statement and Non-GAAP Inconsistencies

**1. Fiscal year 2023 subscription revenue is inconsistent.** The CFO memorandum (Section 4.1) states fiscal year 2023 subscription revenue of $113.4 million, while the audited consolidated statements of operations report $116.4 million. The CFO memorandum's figure also drives an overstated subscription growth rate of 48.7% (the correct rate, using the audited $116.4 million base, is 44.8%). The $3.0 million difference appears to have been shifted to professional services (see Item 2). The Form S-1 uses the audited $116.4 million.

**2. Fiscal year 2023 professional services revenue is inconsistent.** The CFO memorandum (Section 4.1) states fiscal year 2023 professional services revenue of $15.5 million, while the audited financial statements report $12.5 million. The CFO memorandum's figure drives an understated professional services growth rate of 20.6% (the correct rate, using the audited $12.5 million base, is 49.6%). The Form S-1 uses the audited $12.5 million.

**3. Fiscal year 2023 stock-based compensation is inconsistent.** The CFO memorandum's Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation (Section 5.2) uses fiscal year 2023 stock-based compensation of $29.1 million, while the audited financial statements (Note 12(d) and the cash flow statement) report $26.4 million.

**4. Fiscal year 2023 interest expense is inconsistent.** The CFO memorandum's reconciliation uses fiscal year 2023 interest expense of $2.8 million, while the audited financial statements report $3.1 million.

**5. Fiscal year 2023 interest income is inconsistent.** The CFO memorandum's reconciliation uses fiscal year 2023 interest income of $4.6 million, while the audited financial statements report $5.8 million.

**6. Fiscal year 2023 income tax provision is inconsistent.** The CFO memorandum's reconciliation uses fiscal year 2023 income tax provision of $0.2 million, while the audited financial statements report $0.1 million.

**7. Fiscal year 2023 restructuring charge is fabricated.** The CFO memorandum's reconciliation (Section 5.2) adds back $0.6 million of restructuring charges for fiscal year 2023. The audited financial statements (Note 9) reflect no restructuring accrual or charge in fiscal year 2023; the restructuring occurred in the third quarter of fiscal year 2024. There is no support for a fiscal year 2023 restructuring add-back.

**8. Fiscal year 2023 depreciation and amortization is inconsistent.** The CFO memorandum's reconciliation uses fiscal year 2023 D&A of $6.9 million. The audited cash flow statement reports D&A of $7.1 million for fiscal year 2023, and Note 5 reports property and equipment D&A of $5.5 million. The $6.9 million figure matches neither audited figure.

**9. Fiscal year 2023 Adjusted EBITDA is overstated.** Using the CFO memorandum's (incorrect) inputs, fiscal year 2023 Adjusted EBITDA is stated as $7.6 million. Using the audited financial statement inputs (net loss $(27.4)M + D&A $7.1M + SBC $26.4M + interest expense $3.1M − interest income $5.8M + tax $0.1M + restructuring $0), Adjusted EBITDA would be approximately $3.5 million. The $7.6 million figure is overstated by approximately $4.1 million.

**10. Fiscal year 2024 Adjusted EBITDA does not foot.** The CFO memorandum (Section 5.2) and the roadshow presentation (Slides 3, 23, 34, and 35) state fiscal year 2024 Adjusted EBITDA of $22.8 million. However, the stated line items sum to $20.5 million: $(24.8) + $8.7 + $38.2 + $3.1 − $6.2 + $0.3 + $1.2 = $20.5 million. The $2.3 million discrepancy is unexplained. The roadshow notes expressly acknowledge this error. Presenting a non-GAAP reconciliation that does not reconcile would violate Regulation G and Item 10(e) of Regulation S-K and would likely draw an SEC comment letter. The Form S-1 presents $20.5 million, which must be confirmed with the Company.

**11. Fiscal year 2024 Adjusted EBITDA margin is incorrect.** The roadshow (Slide 23) states an Adjusted EBITDA margin of 12.2% (based on the erroneous $22.8 million). Using the correct $20.5 million, the margin is 10.9% ($20.5 / $187.3). This must be corrected.

**12. Condensed balance sheet figures in the CFO memorandum differ from the audited balance sheet.** The CFO memorandum (Section 6.1) states the following as of December 31, 2024, each of which differs from the audited consolidated balance sheet: accounts receivable, net $28.2 million (audited: $29.8 million); prepaid expenses and other current assets $13.2 million (audited: $11.6 million); accounts payable $9.8 million (audited: $8.2 million); accrued liabilities $14.7 million (audited: $12.1 million). The Form S-1 uses the audited figures.

**13. Restructuring description is inconsistent as to headcount and timing.** The CFO memorandum (Section 5.3) describes the fiscal year 2024 restructuring as affecting "approximately 30 positions" in the "second quarter of 2024" in the professional services organization. The audited financial statements (Note 9) describe a workforce reduction affecting "approximately 46 employees" in the "third quarter of 2024." Both the headcount (30 vs. 46) and the timing (Q2 vs. Q3) conflict and must be reconciled.

**14. Professional services are mischaracterized as "high-margin."** The roadshow presentation (Slides 12, 17, 21, and 27) describes professional services as "high-margin and accretive" and projects professional services gross margins of 15–20%. The audited financial statements show fiscal year 2024 professional services revenue of $18.7 million against professional services cost of revenue of $18.8 million, a gross margin of approximately negative 0.5%. The roadshow appendix (Slide 35) correctly shows the negative 0.5% margin, creating an internal inconsistency within the presentation. The "high-margin" characterization is contradicted by the audited financials and must be corrected in all investor-facing materials. The Form S-1 discloses the negative professional services gross margin.

---

## III. Term Loan Inconsistencies

**15. The date of the term loan facility is inconsistent.** The term loan summary states that the Term Loan and Security Agreement is "dated as of December 15, 2022," with a closing date of December 15, 2022, and an initial draw of $40.0 million on the closing date. The audited financial statements (Note 8) state that "in June 2023, the Company entered into a term loan facility." These cannot both be correct. The Form S-1 description of indebtedness and the exhibit list (Exhibit 10.8) must reflect the correct date. This should be confirmed against the executed credit agreement.

**16. The term loan cash flow activity does not reconcile to the balance sheet.** The consolidated statements of cash flows reflect $15.0 million of "proceeds from term loan borrowings" in fiscal year 2023 and $5.0 million of "repayment of term loan" in fiscal year 2024, with no borrowings in fiscal year 2024. The balance sheet, however, reflects a constant $40.0 million term loan balance at both December 31, 2023 and December 31, 2024. If $40.0 million was outstanding at the beginning of fiscal year 2023 and $15.0 million was borrowed with no repayments, the year-end 2023 balance should be $55.0 million, not $40.0 million. If $40.0 million was outstanding at the beginning of fiscal year 2024 and $5.0 million was repaid with no borrowings, the year-end 2024 balance should be $35.0 million, not $40.0 million. This internal inconsistency in the audited financial statements must be resolved with Archer Pryce LLP before filing.

**17. The term loan amortization schedule is inconsistent.** The term loan summary states that the term loan amortizes in quarterly installments of 1.25% of the original drawn amount, or $500,000 per quarter ($2.0 million per year), with the remaining balance due as a bullet at maturity. The audited financial statements (Note 8) reflect future principal repayments of $5.0 million in 2025, $10.0 million in 2026, and $25.0 million in 2027, and the cash flow statement reflects $5.0 million actually repaid in fiscal year 2024. A $2.0 million annual amortization does not produce $5.0 million annual repayments. The amortization schedule in the financial statements must be reconciled to the credit agreement.

**18. The term loan lender is misnamed in the roadshow.** The roadshow presentation (Slides 20, 24, and 27) refers to the lender as "Silicon Valley Credit Partners." The correct lender name, as stated in the term loan summary, the audited financial statements, the CFO memorandum, and the underwriting term sheet, is "Kestridge West Credit Partners." (The term loan summary's abbreviation "SVCP" may be the source of the error.) The roadshow must be corrected.

---

## IV. Equity, Capitalization, and 409A Valuation Inconsistencies

**19. The 409A valuation history is materially inconsistent between the audited financial statements and the equity plan records.** The audited financial statements (Note 12(e)) list the following 409A valuations: June 30, 2022—$7.80; September 30, 2022—$9.50; March 31, 2023—$14.20; September 30, 2023—$16.50; March 31, 2024—$18.20; September 30, 2024—$19.60. The equity plan records (409A Valuations tab) list: September 30, 2018—$0.85; March 31, 2020—$2.40; September 30, 2020—$3.10; December 31, 2021—$7.20; September 30, 2022—$9.50; March 31, 2023—$14.20; December 31, 2023—$16.80; December 31, 2024 (preliminary)—$18.50. Only two dates (September 30, 2022 and March 31, 2023) appear in both. The remaining dates and values are entirely different. This is a material inconsistency affecting the stock-based compensation footnote, the cheap-stock analysis, and the Section 409A compliance disclosure. The correct valuation history must be confirmed with the valuation firm (Meridian Valuation Partners LLC) and the auditor.

**20. The RSU opening balance as of January 1, 2024 is inconsistent.** The audited financial statements (Note 12(c)) report 2,800,000 unvested RSUs as of January 1, 2024. The equity plan records reflect only the 2023 RSU grants (RSU-2023-001, -002, -003), which total 950,000 granted and, after 90,000 forfeitures, 860,000 outstanding. There is no support in the equity plan records for 2,800,000 unvested RSUs at January 1, 2024.

**21. The number of RSUs granted in fiscal year 2024 is inconsistent.** The audited financial statements (Note 12(c)) report 1,400,000 RSUs granted in fiscal year 2024. The equity plan records reflect fiscal year 2024 RSU grants (RSU-2024-001 through RSU-2024-017) totaling 2,450,000 shares. This is a difference of 1,050,000 shares.

**22. The fiscal year 2024 RSU grant-date fair value is inconsistent.** The audited financial statements (Note 12(c)) report fiscal year 2024 RSU grants at a weighted-average grant-date fair value of $18.20 per share. The equity plan records reflect fiscal year 2024 RSU grants at $18.50 per share.

**23. The RSU weighted-average grant-date fair value as of December 31, 2024 is inconsistent.** The audited financial statements (Note 12(c)) report a weighted-average grant-date fair value of $15.40 per share for the 3,200,000 RSUs outstanding as of December 31, 2024. Computing the weighted average from the equity plan grant records (860,000 shares at $14.20 and 2,340,000 shares at $18.50) yields approximately $17.34 per share. These do not match.

**24. The number of option exercises is inconsistent.** The equity plan records (Option Grants tab totals) reflect 60,000 shares exercised in the aggregate. The audited financial statements (Note 12(b)) reflect 1,100,000 options exercised in fiscal year 2023 and 800,000 in fiscal year 2024, for an aggregate of 1,900,000. The equity plan records understate exercises by 1,840,000 shares.

**25. The number of option forfeitures is inconsistent.** The equity plan records reflect 1,100,000 options cancelled/forfeited in the aggregate. The audited financial statements reflect 300,000 forfeitures in fiscal year 2023 and 350,000 in fiscal year 2024, for an aggregate of 650,000.

**26. The equity plan option totals do not internally reconcile.** The Option Grants tab totals row states 18,910,000 granted, 60,000 exercised, 1,100,000 cancelled/forfeited, and 14,750,000 outstanding. However, 18,910,000 − 60,000 − 1,100,000 = 17,750,000, which does not equal the stated 14,750,000 outstanding (a 3,000,000-share gap). In addition, the stated vested shares (17,165,011) plus unvested shares (5,816,209) total 22,981,220, which exceeds both the granted total and the outstanding total. The equity plan records must be reconciled.

**27. The equity plan RSU totals do not internally reconcile.** The RSU Grants tab totals row states 3,380,000 unvested and 3,200,000 outstanding. Summing the individual "Shares Unvested as of 12/31/2024" column yields 3,220,000, which matches neither the stated 3,380,000 nor the stated 3,200,000. The equity plan records must be reconciled.

**28.
