Cheap-stock / Section 409A risk for first-quarter 2023 option grants.** During the three months ended March 31, 2023, the Company granted 2,400,000 stock options at an exercise price of $9.50 per share, based on the September 30, 2022 409A valuation. A subsequent 409A valuation as of March 31, 2023 determined the fair value of the common stock to be $14.20 per share, a 49.5% increase. The grants were made using a valuation that was approximately six months old at the time of grant, and the contemporaneous (March 31, 2023) valuation suggests the $9.50 exercise price may have been below fair market value. This creates (i) a potential Section 409A noncompliance risk (deferred compensation tax consequences for the grantees) and (ii) a potential additional stock-based compensation expense ("cheap stock") that may need to be recognized. This should be analyzed with the valuation firm and the auditor, and disclosed as a risk factor.

**29. The December 31, 2024 409A valuation is preliminary.** The equity plan records indicate that the December 31, 2024 409A valuation (fair value $18.50 per share) is preliminary, with the final report and Board approval expected in February 2025. This valuation is needed for the stock-based compensation footnote in the Form S-1 and for any pre-IPO equity grants. The final valuation must be obtained and Board-approved before filing.

**30. Secondary common stock transactions occurred in 2024.** The equity plan records (409A Valuations tab) note secondary transactions in the third and fourth quarters of 2024 at implied valuations of $18–$20 per share of common stock equivalent. These secondary transactions are relevant to the determination of the fair value of the common stock, to the cheap-stock analysis, and potentially to disclosure. The Company should confirm whether any secondary transactions require disclosure and whether they affect the common stock valuation.

---

## V. Identity and Governance Inconsistencies

**31. The co-founder and CTO's name is inconsistent.** The amended and restated certificate of incorporation (including the Permitted Transferee definition in Section 4.3) and the underwriting term sheet refer to the co-founder and CTO as "Katerina Litvak." The CFO memorandum, the management questionnaire, and the equity plan records refer to her as "Katerina Litvanova." The correct legal name must be confirmed and used consistently throughout the Form S-1, the charter, the underwriting agreement, and all other offering documents. An incorrect name in a registration statement signed under Section 11 liability is a material concern.

**32. The size of the board of directors and Ms. Litvak's director status are inconsistent.** The amended and restated certificate of incorporation (Article V, Section 5.3) provides for an initial board of seven directors, with Katerina Litvak named as a Class III director (alongside Arun Ramaswamy and Adaora Obi). The management questionnaire (Section 6) states that the board consists of six members and that "Katerina Litvanova, Michael D. Hartwell, and Sandra Y. Chen are executive officers but do not serve on the Board." The roadshow presentation is internally inconsistent: Slide 7 implies a six-member board (five non-employee directors plus the CEO), while Slide 30 states "3 of 7 total directors." The Form S-1 must state the correct board composition. If Ms. Litvak is a director, the questionnaire and roadshow must be corrected; if she is not, the charter's classified-board provision (which lists her as a Class III director) must be corrected. This affects the director signatures on the registration statement, the director biographies, the independence analysis, and the controlled-company analysis.

**33. The transfer agent is inconsistent.** The amended and restated bylaws (Article V, Section 5.1) identify the transfer agent and registrar as "Computershare Transfer Co." The CFO memorandum (Section 10, action item 6), the management questionnaire (Open Item 3), and the underwriting term sheet (Section 1 and the lock-up agreement, Section 6) identify the transfer agent as "Oakvale Transfer Transfer Co." (note the doubled "Transfer Transfer"). The correct transfer agent must be confirmed, the bylaws must be corrected if necessary, and the name must be used consistently. The apparent typographical error in "Oakvale Transfer Transfer Co." should also be corrected.

**34. Sandra Y. Chen's title is inconsistent.** The amended and restated certificate of incorporation and bylaws, the management questionnaire, the CFO memorandum, and the roadshow presentation identify Sandra Y. Chen as General Counsel and Corporate Secretary. The equity plan records (Option Grants tab, OPT-2018-002 and OPT-2024-005; RSU Grants tab, RSU-2024-004) identify "Sandra Chen" as "VP Sales (now CRO)" / "CRO." In addition, the equity plan records (OPT-2022-002 and RSU-2024-005) identify "Thomas Brennan" as "General Counsel," a person who does not appear in any other document. Either the equity plan records contain mislabeled grantees (possibly conflating two different individuals), or there are persons not reflected in the management questionnaire. The grantee identities and titles must be reconciled, as they affect the named executive officer disclosures and the executive compensation tables.

**35. The non-employee director RSU grantee names in the equity plan records do not match the actual directors.** The equity plan records (RSU Grants tab, RSU-2024-009 through RSU-2024-013) reflect fiscal year 2024 non-employee director RSU grants of 15,000 shares each to individuals named "Anand Venkatesh," "Claudia Moreno," "David Park," "Soo-Yeon Kwon," and "Emmanuel Obi." The actual non-employee directors are Priya Venkatesh, Thomas J. Moreno, Dr. Helen Park, James Kwon, and Adaora Obi. The first names differ (Anand vs. Priya; Claudia vs. Thomas J.; David vs. Dr. Helen; Soo-Yeon vs. James; Emmanuel vs. Adaora) while the last names match. This is a significant red flag: either the equity plan records contain incorrect grantee names, or the director identities in the management questionnaire are incorrect. Director compensation and beneficial ownership disclosure depend on correct identities and must be reconciled against the stock transfer records.

**36. The named executive officers' equity awards are understated in the management questionnaire.** The management questionnaire discloses only the fiscal year 2024 RSU awards for the named executive officers (180,000 to Mr. Ramaswamy; 150,000 to Dr. Litvak; 120,000 to Mr. Hartwell; 90,000 to Ms. Chen). The equity plan records also reflect fiscal year 2024 supplemental non-qualified stock option grants to each named executive officer (100,000 to Mr. Ramaswamy; 80,000 to Dr. Litvak; 60,000 to Mr. Hartwell; 50,000 to Ms. Chen, each at an exercise price of $16.80 per share). The summary compensation table and outstanding equity award table in the Form S-1 must reflect both the RSU and option awards.

**37. The Series B participant is misnamed in the roadshow.** The roadshow presentation (Slide 29) refers to "Crestview Capital" as a Series B participant. The audited financial statements, the CFO memorandum, the management questionnaire, and the equity plan records all refer to the Series B participant as "Aldersgate Capital." "Crestview Capital" does not appear in any other document. The roadshow must be corrected, and the current ownership status of Aldersgate Capital must be determined (see Item 41).

---

## VI. Beneficial Ownership Inconsistencies

**38. The combined beneficial ownership of the two largest stockholders exceeds total preferred stock.** The beneficial ownership table (CFO memorandum Section 7.1 and management questionnaire Section 4.1) attributes 23,607,067 shares to Graymont Ventures and 15,594,267 shares to Ridgeline Growth Partners, for a combined total of 39,201,334 shares. Because these entities are venture funds that hold only preferred stock (which converts one-for-one into common stock), their combined holdings cannot exceed the 35,416,667 total shares of preferred stock outstanding. The share counts attributed to Graymont and/or Ridgeline appear to be overstated by at least 3,784,667 shares in the aggregate and must be reconciled against the capitalization records and stock transfer records.

**39. The beneficial ownership table uses inconsistent numerator and denominator bases.** The table computes each holder's percentage against a denominator of 105,366,667 fully diluted shares (which includes all 14,750,000 outstanding options and all 3,200,000 outstanding RSUs). However, the share counts attributed to individuals include only vested options and RSUs (or those vesting within 60 days), not all outstanding options and RSUs. SEC beneficial ownership disclosure generally requires the numerator and denominator to be on a consistent basis. The table should be recomputed on a consistent basis (typically, the denominator should reflect the same securities included in the numerator, plus the shares included in other holders' numerators).

**40. The founders' Class B share counts do not reconcile to their beneficial ownership share counts.** The Class B common stock holdings are stated as 12,200,000 shares (Mr. Ramaswamy) and 8,600,000 shares (Dr. Litvak), totaling 20,800,000. The beneficial ownership table attributes 12,222,533 shares to Mr. Ramaswamy and 9,693,733 shares to Dr. Litvak. The differences (22,533 and 1,093,733, respectively) presumably reflect vested options and RSUs, but the amounts should be confirmed against the equity plan records and disclosed transparently.

**41. Aldersgate Capital's current ownership is unknown.** Aldersgate Capital participated in the Series B financing (April 2020) but does not appear in any current beneficial ownership disclosure. No questionnaire respondent reports Aldersgate Capital as a current stockholder. If Aldersgate Capital holds 5% or more of any class of the Company's securities, it must be included in the principal stockholders table (Item 403 of Regulation S-K) and may be required to execute a lock-up agreement. The Company must confirm Aldersgate Capital's current holdings against the stock transfer records and obtain a completed D&O questionnaire or equivalent disclosure if Aldersgate Capital remains a 5% holder. The principal stockholders table cannot be finalized until this is resolved.

**42. The post-IPO ownership estimates in the CFO memorandum are inconsistent with the beneficial ownership share counts.** The CFO memorandum (Section 7.2) estimates post-IPO ownership of approximately 19.3% for Graymont Ventures and 12.7% for Ridgeline Growth Partners (based on 102,416,667 post-IPO shares). However, the stated share counts (23,607,067 and 15,594,267) represent 23.05% and 15.23% of 102,416,667 shares, respectively. The Section 7.2 percentages imply lower share counts (approximately 19.8 million and 13.0 million) than the Section 7.1 table states. This is consistent with the overstatement identified in Item 38 and must be reconciled.

---

## VII. Related Party and Disclosure Inconsistencies

**43. The Vantara Logistics related party transaction is inconsistently disclosed.** The Vantara Logistics, Inc. SaaS license agreement ($1.2 million annual contract value, three-year term, entered into April 2023) is a related party transaction because Vantara is a portfolio company of Graymont Ventures, whose Managing Partner, Priya Venkatesh, serves on the Company's board and on Vantara's board. Ms. Venkatesh disclosed this transaction in her director questionnaire. The CFO did not identify it as a related party transaction in his questionnaire, and the CFO memorandum (Exhibit B) and the roadshow presentation (Slide 14) present Vantara as an ordinary customer (including as a customer case study) without any related-party notation. All investor-facing materials must be updated to reflect Item 404 of Regulation S-K disclosure, including the Graymont affiliation, Ms. Venkatesh's dual board roles, the Audit Committee approval, and Ms. Venkatesh's recusal. The Form S-1 includes the Item 404 disclosure.

**44. The Vantara transaction may require lender notification under the term loan.** The term loan agreement (Section 7.2) requires prior written notification to the lender for affiliate transactions exceeding $500,000 in value. The Vantara SaaS agreement has an annual contract value of $1.2 million, which exceeds this threshold. The Company should confirm whether the required lender notification was provided and, if not, whether a cure is needed. This should be disclosed if material.

---

## VIII. Timing Inconsistencies

**45. The Form S-1 filing date is inconsistent.** The audited financial statements (Note 17, Subsequent Events) state that "in February 2025, the Company filed a Registration Statement on Form S-1 with the Securities and Exchange Commission." The CFO memorandum (dated February 14, 2025) states that the Company "anticipate[s] filing the initial Form S-1 in March 2025," and the underwriting term sheet and roadshow presentation are dated March 2025. The Form S-1 cannot have been filed in February 2025 if the target filing date is March 2025. The subsequent events note must be corrected to reflect the actual filing date (or intended filing date), and the auditor's report date must be consistent.

**46. The auditor's report date is inconsistent with the stated filing date.** The auditor's report is dated "March __, 2025." The subsequent events note states that the Form S-1 was filed in February 2025 and that subsequent events were evaluated through "March __, 2025." An auditor's report in a registration statement must be dated no later than the date of the registration statement. If the registration statement is filed in February 2025, a March-dated audit report is inconsistent. The audit report date, the subsequent events evaluation date, and the filing date must be aligned.

---

## IX. Use of Proceeds Inconsistencies

**47. The use of proceeds allocation is inconsistent across documents.** The CFO memorandum (Section 3.2) specifies a particular allocation: approximately 35% to R&D, 25% to sales and marketing, 15% to debt repayment, 10% to acquisitions, and the remainder to general corporate purposes. The roadshow presentation (Slide 27) specifies a different allocation: approximately 40% to R&D, 30% to sales and marketing, 15% to acquisitions, 10% to working capital/general corporate purposes, and 5% to term loan repayment. The underwriting term sheet (Section 4) states that the Company "has not determined the specific allocation of net proceeds among these uses." For an emerging growth company, a general use of proceeds disclosure is permissible, but the inconsistent percentages across the Company's materials must be reconciled, and the Form S-1 should not state specific allocations that the Company has not in fact determined.

**48. The pro forma as-adjusted balance sheet and dilution analysis do not reflect the stated debt repayment.** The CFO memorandum's pro forma as-adjusted capitalization table (Exhibit A) and dilution analysis (Section 8) assume the full $331.8 million of net proceeds is added to net tangible book value, with the $40.0 million term loan remaining outstanding. If a portion of the proceeds is in fact used to repay the term loan (15% per the CFO memorandum, or 5% per the roadshow), the pro forma as-adjusted balance sheet and the dilution analysis should reflect the reduced cash and reduced debt. The current presentation is internally inconsistent with the stated use of proceeds.

---

## X. Governance, Controlled Company, and Structural Risks

**49. The Board has not formally determined whether to rely on the controlled company exemption.** Following the IPO, the co-founders will hold approximately 71.8% of the voting power, making the Company a "controlled company" under Nasdaq Listing Rule 5615(c). No board resolution is on record regarding whether the Company will rely on the controlled company exemptions (from the majority-independent-board, independent-compensation-committee, and independent-nominating-committee requirements). The director questionnaire responses reflect awareness but no definitive election. As of the date of the questionnaires, only three of six (or three of seven, depending on the resolution of Item 32) directors are independent. The Board must adopt a formal resolution, and the Form S-1 must disclose whether the Company will rely on the exemption and the resulting governance implications. The Form S-1 risk factors and corporate governance disclosure assume the exemption will be relied upon, subject to Board confirmation.

**50. The dual-class structure has no sunset provision.** The Class B common stock automatically converts into Class A common stock only upon transfer to a non-permitted transferee or upon the death or incapacity of the holder. There is no time-based sunset (e.g., a fixed number of years after the IPO) and no ownership-percentage-based sunset. The dual-class structure and the resulting concentration of voting control may therefore persist indefinitely. This is disclosed as a risk factor in the Form S-1. The Company should consider whether to adopt a sunset provision, as institutional investors and proxy advisors increasingly favor sunsets, and certain index providers exclude multi-class structures.

**51. The federal forum selection clause for Securities Act claims should be disclosed.** The charter (Article X, Section 10.2) designates the federal district courts as the exclusive forum for Securities Act claims. This provision is enforceable under Delaware law following *Salzberg v. Sciabacucchi* (Del. 2020), but it affects the ability of stockholders to bring Securities Act claims (including Section 11 claims) in state court, which under *Cyan Inc. v. Beaver County Employees Retirement Fund* (2018) would otherwise be concurrently available in state and federal court. This is disclosed as a risk factor in the Form S-1.

**52. The anti-takeover provisions should be disclosed.** The charter and bylaws contain provisions that may discourage, delay, or prevent a change of control, including: a classified (staggered) board; the inability of Class A holders to act by written consent; the inability of stockholders to call special meetings; board-filled vacancies; removal of directors only for cause and only by a 66 2/3% vote; and supermajority (66 2/3%) voting requirements to amend certain provisions of the charter and bylaws. These are disclosed as risk factors in the Form S-1.

**53. The corporate opportunities renunciation should be disclosed.** The charter (Article XI) renounces the Company's interest in business opportunities presented to directors who are partners or employees of investment funds, expressly including Graymont Ventures and Ridgeline Growth Partners and their affiliates and portfolio companies. This creates a potential conflict of interest given that two directors are affiliated with these funds and that the Company has an existing related party customer relationship with a Graymont portfolio company (Vantara). This should be disclosed in the Form S-1.

---

## XI. Litigation, Tax, and Other Risks

**54. The Optivance patent litigation could trigger a term loan default.** The Optivance litigation (Case No. 6:24-cv-00831, E.D. Tex.) carries an estimated exposure of $5.0 million to $20.0 million in the event of an adverse judgment. Under the term loan agreement (Section 8.1(h)), one or more judgments for the payment of money in excess of $5.0 million in the aggregate that remain undischarged for 60 days constitute an event of default. An adverse judgment at the upper end of the range could therefore result in acceleration of the term loan if not bonded, appealed, or stayed. This is disclosed as a risk factor in the Form S-1.

**55. The Section 382 NOL limitation risk should be disclosed.** As of December 31, 2024, the Company had approximately $228.0 million of federal NOL carryforwards and $166.0 million of state NOL carryforwards. The Company has not completed a Section 382 study. The IPO and other equity transactions could trigger an "ownership change" under Section 382 of the Internal Revenue Code, which would limit the Company's ability to use its NOLs to offset future taxable income. This is disclosed as a risk factor in the Form S-1.

**56. The term loan is secured by substantially all of the Company's assets, including its intellectual property.** The first-priority lien covers substantially all assets, including the 47 issued U.S. patents, 12 pending applications, 8 foreign patents, and the registered trademarks, as well as 65% of the voting equity of the UK subsidiary. The encumbrance of the Company's core intellectual property is a significant risk and is disclosed in the Form S-1.

**57. The term loan's key-person and change-of-control provisions should be disclosed.** The term loan provides that a change of control occurs if Arun Ramaswamy ceases to serve as CEO (unless a successor acceptable to the lender is appointed within 90 days) and if existing stockholders cease to hold at least 50% of voting power (including following an IPO). While the IPO as structured does not trigger the voting-power threshold (the co-founders retain 71.8%), the loss of Mr. Ramaswamy as CEO could trigger a default. This is disclosed in the Form S-1.

**58. The 30-day pre-offering notice to the lender must be satisfied.** The term loan agreement (Section 7.14) requires the Company to provide the lender with written notice not less than 30 days before the consummation of the IPO, including the anticipated date, gross proceeds, use of proceeds, and a pro forma capitalization table. Failure to provide this notice is an event of default (subject to a 10-business-day cure). This notice must be delivered on the offering timeline and is disclosed in the Form S-1.

**59. The accumulated deficit and stockholders' deficit should be disclosed as risks.** As of December 31, 2024, the Company had an accumulated deficit of $312.6 million and a total stockholders' deficit of $288.0 million. Although the auditor issued an unqualified opinion and management believes existing liquidity is sufficient for at least 12 months, the Company's history of losses and capital needs are disclosed as risk factors.

**60. Customer concentration should be disclosed.** The Company's largest customer, Harmon Industries Inc., represented approximately 8.2% of fiscal year 2024 revenue, and the top 10 customers represented 31.7%. This concentration is disclosed as a risk factor in the Form S-1.

---

## XII. Open Items Requiring Follow-Up Before Filing

**61. Director ages must be obtained.** The management questionnaire does not provide ages for the non-employee directors (Venkatesh, Moreno, Park, Kwon, and Obi), each shown as "[●]." Item 401 of Regulation S-K requires disclosure of the age of each director and executive officer. The ages must be confirmed.

**62. Lock-up agreements must bind the fund-level entities.** The shares beneficially owned by Graymont Ventures and Ridgeline Growth Partners are held at the fund level (Graymont Ventures Fund III, L.P. and Fund IV, L.P.; Ridgeline Growth Partners Fund V, L.P.), not in the individual names of the directors who serve as their representatives. The lock-up agreements must be executed by the fund-level entities, not merely by Ms. Venkatesh and Mr. Kwon in their individual capacities. The underwriting agreement and lock-up letter forms must be cross-checked to ensure proper coverage.

**63. The underwriting discount split is undetermined.** The underwriting term sheet (Section 2) leaves the split of the underwriting discount between Pinnacle Ridge Capital and Stonebridge Greer & Co. as "[●]% (to be agreed)." This must be finalized and must comply with FINRA Rule 5110.

**64. D&O insurance must be placed.** The CFO memorandum (action item 9) notes that directors' and officers' liability insurance with appropriate public company coverage limits must be placed. This is a condition to closing and a Nasdaq listing consideration.

**65. Principal accountant fees must be obtained.** The source documents do not provide the audit, audit-related, tax, and other fees paid to Archer Pryce LLP. These amounts are required for the "Principal Accountant Fees and Services" disclosure (Item 9 of Regulation S-K) and must be obtained from the Company.

**66. The 2024 Equity Incentive Plan and ESPP documents must be filed as exhibits and reviewed.** The 2024 Equity Incentive Plan (including the double-trigger change-of-control acceleration and the 2017 Plan roll-over) and the 2024 ESPP (including the evergreen provision and the Section 423 compliance features) must be filed as exhibits and reviewed for consistency with the Form S-1 disclosures. The RSU liquidity-event (double-trigger) settlement condition should be disclosed, as it affects the timing of share settlements.

**67. The Investor Rights Agreement registration rights must be disclosed.** The Investor Rights Agreement (June 2023) provides demand registration rights and S-3 piggyback registration rights commencing 180 days after the IPO. These registration rights create potential future selling pressure and should be disclosed in the Form S-1.

**68. India operations should be confirmed.** The Company maintains an office in Bangalore, India, with employees, but the audited financial statements identify only Crestline Software UK Ltd. as a consolidated subsidiary. The Company should confirm whether there is an Indian subsidiary or branch that should be consolidated or otherwise disclosed, and whether any additional tax or regulatory disclosures are required.

**69. Interim financial statements may be required.** The audited financial statements cover fiscal years 2023 and 2024 (two years), which is sufficient for an emerging growth company. However, if the offering is not completed before the end of the first quarter of fiscal year 2025, interim financial statements for the period ended March 31, 2025 (and the comparable prior-year period) will be required, and the prospectus will need to be updated. The offering timeline should be managed with this in mind.

**70. Emerging growth company status and accommodations should be confirmed and disclosed.** The Company represents that it qualifies as an emerging growth company. With fiscal year 2024 revenue of $187.3 million (below the $1.235 billion threshold) and this being its first registered equity offering, the Company appears to qualify. The Form S-1 should disclose the EGC accommodations the Company intends to elect (including reduced executive compensation disclosure, exemption from SOX 404(b) auditor attestation, and the extended transition period for new accounting standards).

---

## XIII. Summary of Priority

The following items are the highest priority for resolution before the Form S-1 is filed, because they affect the accuracy of the audited financial statements, the correctness of named persons and entities, or the Company's compliance with its debt covenants and SEC disclosure requirements:

- Items 10, 16, and 17 (Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation error; term loan cash-flow/balance-sheet reconciliation; term loan amortization) — these affect the accuracy of the financial statements and non-GAAP disclosure and may require involvement of Archer Pryce LLP.
- Items 19, 20, 21, 24, 25, 26, and 27 (409A valuation history and equity award reconciliation) — these affect the stock-based compensation footnote and must be reconciled among the auditor, the valuation firm, and the equity plan records.
- Items 31, 32, 33, 34, and 35 (name, board size, transfer agent, and grantee identity inconsistencies) — these affect the correctness of the registration statement and the persons signing it.
- Items 38, 41, and 42 (beneficial ownership reconciliation and Aldersgate Capital) — these affect the principal stockholders table, which cannot be finalized until resolved.
- Items 43 and 44 (Vantara related party disclosure and lender notification) — these affect Item 404 disclosure and term loan compliance.
- Items 45 and 46 (filing date and auditor's report date) — these affect the timing and validity of the registration statement.
- Item 49 (controlled company exemption election) — this requires a formal board resolution and drives the corporate governance disclosure.
- Item 58 (30-day lender notice) — this is a covenant obligation on the offering timeline.

We are available to discuss any of the foregoing and to assist in their resolution.

Respectfully submitted,

[Drafting Counsel]", "file_path": "issues-memorandum.md"}

arguments:
{"content": "# ISSUES MEMORANDUM

**TO:** Sarah K. Norden, Esq. and James T. Okafor, Esq., Thorncroft & Wexler LLP; Michael D. Hartwell, Chief Financial Officer; Sandra Y. Chen, General Counsel
**FROM:** [Drafting Counsel]
**DATE:** [●], 2025
**RE:** Inconsistencies, Disclosure Gaps, and Risks Identified in Connection with the Form S-1 Registration Statement for the Proposed Initial Public Offering of Crestline Software, Inc.
**PRIVILEGED AND CONFIDENTIAL — PREPARED AT THE DIRECTION OF COUNSEL**

## I. Introduction and Scope

This memorandum identifies inconsistencies, disclosure gaps, and risks identified during our review of the source documents made available in connection with the preparation of the Registration Statement on Form S-1 for the proposed initial public offering ("IPO") of Crestline Software, Inc. (the "Company"). The documents reviewed include: (1) the audited consolidated financial statements for fiscal years 2023 and 2024; (2) the CFO's IPO readiness memorandum dated February 14, 2025; (3) the amended and restated certificate of incorporation and bylaws; (4) the management (director and officer) questionnaire responses; (5) the term loan summary; (6) the underwriting term sheet and form of lock-up agreement; (7) the equity plan summary and grant records; and (8) the draft roadshow presentation.

Each item below is stated separately so that it can be tracked to resolution. Items are grouped by category for ease of reference, but each numbered item is a distinct issue. Where a figure in the Company's internal materials conflicts with the audited financial statements, the audited financial statements control for purposes of the Form S-1, and the conflict is flagged here for correction of the internal materials and for confirmation that the audited figures are correct.

This memorandum is not exhaustive of all matters that may arise during the SEC review process, and additional issues may be identified as drafting continues.

---

## II. Financial Statement and Non-GAAP Inconsistencies

**1. Fiscal year 2023 subscription revenue is inconsistent.** The CFO memorandum (Section 4.1) states fiscal year 2023 subscription revenue of $113.4 million, while the audited consolidated statements of operations report $116.4 million. The CFO memorandum's figure also drives an overstated subscription growth rate of 48.7% (the correct rate, using the audited $116.4 million base, is 44.8%). The $3.0 million difference appears to have been shifted to professional services (see Item 2). The Form S-1 uses the audited $116.4 million.

**2. Fiscal year 2023 professional services revenue is inconsistent.** The CFO memorandum (Section 4.1) states fiscal year 2023 professional services revenue of $15.5 million, while the audited financial statements report $12.5 million. The CFO memorandum's figure drives an understated professional services growth rate of 20.6% (the correct rate, using the audited $12.5 million base, is 49.6%). The Form S-1 uses the audited $12.5 million.

**3. Fiscal year 2023 stock-based compensation is inconsistent.** The CFO memorandum's Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation (Section 5.2) uses fiscal year 2023 stock-based compensation of $29.1 million, while the audited financial statements (Note 12(d) and the cash flow statement) report $26.4 million.

**4. Fiscal year 2023 interest expense is inconsistent.** The CFO memorandum's reconciliation uses fiscal year 2023 interest expense of $2.8 million, while the audited financial statements report $3.1 million.

**5. Fiscal year 2023 interest income is inconsistent.** The CFO memorandum's reconciliation uses fiscal year 2023 interest income of $4.6 million, while the audited financial statements report $5.8 million.

**6. Fiscal year 2023 income tax provision is inconsistent.** The CFO memorandum's reconciliation uses fiscal year 2023 income tax provision of $0.2 million, while the audited financial statements report $0.1 million.

**7. Fiscal year 2023 restructuring charge is fabricated.** The CFO memorandum's reconciliation (Section 5.2) adds back $0.6 million of restructuring charges for fiscal year 2023. The audited financial statements (Note 9) reflect no restructuring accrual or charge in fiscal year 2023; the restructuring occurred in the third quarter of fiscal year 2024. There is no support for a fiscal year 2023 restructuring add-back.

**8. Fiscal year 2023 depreciation and amortization is inconsistent.** The CFO memorandum's reconciliation uses fiscal year 2023 D&A of $6.9 million. The audited cash flow statement reports D&A of $7.1 million for fiscal year 2023, and Note 5 reports property and equipment D&A of $5.5 million. The $6.9 million figure matches neither audited figure.

**9. Fiscal year 2023 Adjusted EBITDA is overstated.** Using the CFO memorandum's (incorrect) inputs, fiscal year 2023 Adjusted EBITDA is stated as $7.6 million. Using the audited financial statement inputs (net loss $(27.4)M + D&A $7.1M + SBC $26.4M + interest expense $3.1M − interest income $5.8M + tax $0.1M + restructuring $0), Adjusted EBITDA would be approximately $3.5 million. The $7.6 million figure is overstated by approximately $4.1 million.

**10. Fiscal year 2024 Adjusted EBITDA does not foot.** The CFO memorandum (Section 5.2) and the roadshow presentation (Slides 3, 23, 34, and 35) state fiscal year 2024 Adjusted EBITDA of $22.8 million. However, the stated line items sum to $20.5 million: $(24.8) + $8.7 + $38.2 + $3.1 − $6.2 + $0.3 + $1.2 = $20.5 million. The $2.3 million discrepancy is unexplained. The roadshow notes expressly acknowledge this error. Presenting a non-GAAP reconciliation that does not reconcile would violate Regulation G and Item 10(e) of Regulation S-K and would likely draw an SEC comment letter. The Form S-1 presents $20.5 million, which must be confirmed with the Company.

**11. Fiscal year 2024 Adjusted EBITDA margin is incorrect.** The roadshow (Slide 23) states an Adjusted EBITDA margin of 12.2% (based on the erroneous $22.8 million). Using the correct $20.5 million, the margin is 10.9% ($20.5 / $187.3). This must be corrected.

**12. Condensed balance sheet figures in the CFO memorandum differ from the audited balance sheet.** The CFO memorandum (Section 6.1) states the following as of December 31, 2024, each of which differs from the audited consolidated balance sheet: accounts receivable, net $28.2 million (audited: $29.8 million); prepaid expenses and other current assets $13.2 million (audited: $11.6 million); accounts payable $9.8 million (audited: $8.2 million); accrued liabilities $14.7 million (audited: $12.1 million). The Form S-1 uses the audited figures.

**13. Restructuring description is inconsistent as to headcount and timing.** The CFO memorandum (Section 5.3) describes the fiscal year 2024 restructuring as affecting "approximately 30 positions" in the "second quarter of 2024" in the professional services organization. The audited financial statements (Note 9) describe a workforce reduction affecting "approximately 46 employees" in the "third quarter of 2024." Both the headcount (30 vs. 46) and the timing (Q2 vs. Q3) conflict and must be reconciled.

**14. Professional services are mischaracterized as "high-margin."** The roadshow presentation (Slides 12, 17, 21, and 27) describes professional services as "high-margin and accretive" and projects professional services gross margins of 15–20%. The audited financial statements show fiscal year 2024 professional services revenue of $18.7 million against professional services cost of revenue of $18.8 million, a gross margin of approximately negative 0.5%. The roadshow appendix (Slide 35) correctly shows the negative 0.5% margin, creating an internal inconsistency within the presentation. The "high-margin" characterization is contradicted by the audited financials and must be corrected in all investor-facing materials. The Form S-1 discloses the negative professional services gross margin.

---

## III. Term Loan Inconsistencies

**15. The date of the term loan facility is inconsistent.** The term loan summary states that the Term Loan and Security Agreement is "dated as of December 15, 2022," with a closing date of December 15, 2022, and an initial draw of $40.0 million on the closing date. The audited financial statements (Note 8) state that "in June 2023, the Company entered into a term loan facility." These cannot both be correct. The Form S-1 description of indebtedness and the exhibit list (Exhibit 10.8) must reflect the correct date. This should be confirmed against the executed credit agreement.

**16. The term loan cash flow activity does not reconcile to the balance sheet.** The consolidated statements of cash flows reflect $15.0 million of "proceeds from term loan borrowings" in fiscal year 2023 and $5.0 million of "repayment of term loan" in fiscal year 2024, with no borrowings in fiscal year 2024. The balance sheet, however, reflects a constant $40.0 million term loan balance at both December 31, 2023 and December 31, 2024. If $40.0 million was outstanding at the beginning of fiscal year 2023 and $15.0 million was borrowed with no repayments, the year-end 2023 balance should be $55.0 million, not $40.0 million. If $40.0 million was outstanding at the beginning of fiscal year 2024 and $5.0 million was repaid with no borrowings, the year-end 2024 balance should be $35.0 million, not $40.0 million. This internal inconsistency in the audited financial statements must be resolved with Archer Pryce LLP before filing.

**17. The term loan amortization schedule is inconsistent.** The term loan summary states that the term loan amortizes in quarterly installments of 1.25% of the original drawn amount, or $500,000 per quarter ($2.0 million per year), with the remaining balance due as a bullet at maturity. The audited financial statements (Note 8) reflect future principal repayments of $5.0 million in 2025, $10.0 million in 2026, and $25.0 million in 2027, and the cash flow statement reflects $5.0 million actually repaid in fiscal year 2024. A $2.0 million annual amortization does not produce $5.0 million annual repayments. The amortization schedule in the financial statements must be reconciled to the credit agreement.

**18. The term loan lender is misnamed in the roadshow.** The roadshow presentation (Slides 20, 24, and 27) refers to the lender as "Silicon Valley Credit Partners." The correct lender name, as stated in the term loan summary, the audited financial statements, the CFO memorandum, and the underwriting term sheet, is "Kestridge West Credit Partners." (The term loan summary's abbreviation "SVCP" may be the source of the error.) The roadshow must be corrected.

---

## IV. Equity, Capitalization, and 409A Valuation Inconsistencies

**19. The 409A valuation history is materially inconsistent between the audited financial statements and the equity plan records.** The audited financial statements (Note 12(e)) list the following 409A valuations: June 30, 2022—$7.80; September 30, 2022—$9.50; March 31, 2023—$14.20; September 30, 2023—$16.50; March 31, 2024—$18.20; September 30, 2024—$19.60. The equity plan records (409A Valuations tab) list: September 30, 2018—$0.85; March 31, 2020—$2.40; September 30, 2020—$3.10; December 31, 2021—$7.20; September 30, 2022—$9.50; March 31, 2023—$14.20; December 31, 2023—$16.80; December 31, 2024 (preliminary)—$18.50. Only two dates (September 30, 2022 and March 31, 2023) appear in both. The remaining dates and values are entirely different. This is a material inconsistency affecting the stock-based compensation footnote, the cheap-stock analysis, and the Section 409A compliance disclosure. The correct valuation history must be confirmed with the valuation firm (Meridian Valuation Partners LLC) and the auditor.

**20. The RSU opening balance as of January 1, 2024 is inconsistent.** The audited financial statements (Note 12(c)) report 2,800,000 unvested RSUs as of January 1, 2024. The equity plan records reflect only the 2023 RSU grants (RSU-2023-001, -002, -003), which total 950,000 granted and, after 90,000 forfeitures, 860,000 outstanding. There is no support in the equity plan records for 2,800,000 unvested RSUs at January 1, 2024.

**21. The number of RSUs granted in fiscal year 2024 is inconsistent.** The audited financial statements (Note 12(c)) report 1,400,000 RSUs granted in fiscal year 2024. The equity plan records reflect fiscal year 2024 RSU grants (RSU-2024-001 through RSU-2024-017) totaling 2,450,000 shares. This is a difference of 1,050,000 shares.

**22. The fiscal year 2024 RSU grant-date fair value is inconsistent.** The audited financial statements (Note 12(c)) report fiscal year 2024 RSU grants at a weighted-average grant-date fair value of $18.20 per share. The equity plan records reflect fiscal year 2024 RSU grants at $18.50 per share.

**23. The RSU weighted-average grant-date fair value as of December 31, 2024 is inconsistent.** The audited financial statements (Note 12(c)) report a weighted-average grant-date fair value of $15.40 per share for the 3,200,000 RSUs outstanding as of December 31, 2024. Computing the weighted average from the equity plan grant records (860,000 shares at $14.20 and 2,340,000 shares at $18.50) yields approximately $17.34 per share. These do not match.

**24. The number of option exercises is inconsistent.** The equity plan records (Option Grants tab totals) reflect 60,000 shares exercised in the aggregate. The audited financial statements (Note 12(b)) reflect 1,100,000 options exercised in fiscal year 2023 and 800,000 in fiscal year 2024, for an aggregate of 1,900,000. The equity plan records understate exercises by 1,840,000 shares.

**25. The number of option forfeitures is inconsistent.** The equity plan records reflect 1,100,000 options cancelled/forfeited in the aggregate. The audited financial statements reflect 300,000 forfeitures in fiscal year 2023 and 350,000 in fiscal year 2024, for an aggregate of 650,000.

**26. The equity plan option totals do not internally reconcile.** The Option Grants tab totals row states 18,910,000 granted, 60,000 exercised, 1,100,000 cancelled/forfeited, and 14,750,000 outstanding. However, 18,910,000 − 60,000 − 1,100,000 = 17,750,000, which does not equal the stated 14,750,000 outstanding (a 3,000,000-share gap). In addition, the stated vested shares (17,165,011) plus unvested shares (5,816,209) total 22,981,220, which exceeds both the granted total and the outstanding total. The equity plan records must be reconciled.

**27. The equity plan RSU totals do not internally reconcile.** The RSU Grants tab totals row states 3,380,000 unvested and 3,200,000 outstanding. Summing the individual "Shares Unvested as of 12/31/2024" column yields 3,220,000, which matches neither the stated 3,380,000 nor the stated 3,200,000. The equity plan records must be reconciled.

**28. Cheap-stock / Section 409A risk for first-quarter 2023 option grants.** During the three months ended March 31, 2023, the Company granted 2,400,000 stock options at an exercise price of $9.50 per share, based on the September 30, 2022 409A valuation. A subsequent 409A valuation as of March 31, 2023 determined the fair value of the common stock to be $14.20 per share, a 49.5% increase. The grants were made using a valuation that was approximately six months old at the time of grant, and the contemporaneous (March 31, 2023) valuation suggests the $9.50 exercise price may have been below fair market value. This creates (i) a potential Section 409A noncompliance risk (deferred compensation tax consequences for the grantees) and (ii) a potential additional stock-based compensation expense ("cheap stock") that may need to be recognized. This should be analyzed with the valuation firm and the auditor, and disclosed as a risk factor.

**29. The December 31, 2024 409A valuation is preliminary.** The equity plan records indicate that the December 31, 2024 409A valuation (fair value $18.50 per share) is preliminary, with the final report and Board approval expected in February 2025. This valuation is needed for the stock-based compensation footnote in the Form S-1 and for any pre-IPO equity grants. The final valuation must be obtained and Board-approved before filing.

**30. Secondary common stock transactions occurred in 2024.** The equity plan records (409A Valuations tab) note secondary transactions in the third and fourth quarters of 2024 at implied valuations of $18–$20 per share of common stock equivalent. These secondary transactions are relevant to the determination of the fair value of the common stock, to the cheap-stock analysis, and potentially to disclosure. The Company should confirm whether any secondary transactions require disclosure and whether they affect the common stock valuation.

---

## V. Identity and Governance Inconsistencies

**31. The co-founder and CTO's name is inconsistent.** The amended and restated certificate of incorporation (including the Permitted Transferee definition in Section 4.3) and the underwriting term sheet refer to the co-founder and CTO as "Katerina Litvak." The CFO memorandum, the management questionnaire, and the equity plan records refer to her as "Katerina Litvanova." The correct legal name must be confirmed and used consistently throughout the Form S-1, the charter, the underwriting agreement, and all other offering documents. An incorrect name in a registration statement signed under Section 11 liability is a material concern.

**32. The size of the board of directors and Ms. Litvak's director status are inconsistent.** The amended and restated certificate of incorporation (Article V, Section 5.3) provides for an initial board of seven directors, with Katerina Litvak named as a Class III director (alongside Arun Ramaswamy and Adaora Obi). The management questionnaire (Section 6) states that the board consists of six members and that "Katerina Litvanova, Michael D. Hartwell, and Sandra Y. Chen are executive officers but do not serve on the Board." The roadshow presentation is internally inconsistent: Slide 7 implies a six-member board (five non-employee directors plus the CEO), while Slide 30 states "3 of 7 total directors." The Form S-1 must state the correct board composition. If Ms. Litvak is a director, the questionnaire and roadshow must be corrected; if she is not, the charter's classified-board provision (which lists her as a Class III director) must be corrected. This affects the director signatures on the registration statement, the director biographies, the independence analysis, and the controlled-company analysis.

**33. The transfer agent is inconsistent.** The amended and restated bylaws (Article V, Section 5.1) identify the transfer agent and registrar as "Computershare Transfer Co." The CFO memorandum (Section 10, action item 6), the management questionnaire (Open Item 3), and the underwriting term sheet (Section 1 and the lock-up agreement, Section 6) identify the transfer agent as "Oakvale Transfer Transfer Co." (note the doubled "Transfer Transfer"). The correct transfer agent must be confirmed, the bylaws must be corrected if necessary, and the name must be used consistently. The apparent typographical error in "Oakvale Transfer Transfer Co." should also be corrected.

**34. Sandra Y. Chen's title is inconsistent.** The amended and restated certificate of incorporation and bylaws, the management questionnaire, the CFO memorandum, and the roadshow presentation identify Sandra Y. Chen as General Counsel and Corporate Secretary. The equity plan records (Option Grants tab, OPT-2018-002 and OPT-2024-005; RSU Grants tab, RSU-2024-004) identify "Sandra Chen" as "VP Sales (now CRO)" / "CRO." In addition, the equity plan records (OPT-2022-002 and RSU-2024-005) identify "Thomas Brennan" as "General Counsel," a person who does not appear in any other document. Either the equity plan records contain mislabeled grantees (possibly conflating two different individuals), or there are persons not reflected in the management questionnaire. The grantee identities and titles must be reconciled, as they affect the named executive officer disclosures and the executive compensation tables.

**35. The non-employee director RSU grantee names in the equity plan records do not match the actual directors.** The equity plan records (RSU Grants tab, RSU-2024-009 through RSU-2024-013) reflect fiscal year 2024 non-employee director RSU grants of 15,000 shares each to individuals named "Anand Venkatesh," "Claudia Moreno," "David Park," "Soo-Yeon Kwon," and "Emmanuel Obi." The actual non-employee directors are Priya Venkatesh, Thomas J. Moreno, Dr. Helen Park, James Kwon, and Adaora Obi. The first names differ (Anand vs. Priya; Claudia vs. Thomas J.; David vs. Dr. Helen; Soo-Yeon vs. James; Emmanuel vs. Adaora) while the last names match. This is a significant red flag: either the equity plan records contain incorrect grantee names, or the director identities in the management questionnaire are incorrect. Director compensation and beneficial ownership disclosure depend on correct identities and must be reconciled against the stock transfer records.

**36. The named executive officers' equity awards are understated in the management questionnaire.** The management questionnaire discloses only the fiscal year 2024 RSU awards for the named executive officers (180,000 to Mr. Ramaswamy; 150,000 to Dr. Litvak; 120,000 to Mr. Hartwell; 90,000 to Ms. Chen). The equity plan records also reflect fiscal year 2024 supplemental non-qualified stock option grants to each named executive officer (100,000 to Mr. Ramaswamy; 80,000 to Dr. Litvak; 60,000 to Mr. Hartwell; 50,000 to Ms. Chen, each at an exercise price of $16.80 per share). The summary compensation table and outstanding equity award table in the Form S-1 must reflect both the RSU and option awards.

**37. The Series B participant is misnamed in the roadshow.** The roadshow presentation (Slide 29) refers to "Crestview Capital" as a Series B participant. The audited financial statements, the CFO memorandum, the management questionnaire, and the equity plan records all refer to the Series B participant as "Aldersgate Capital." "Crestview Capital" does not appear in any other document. The roadshow must be corrected, and the current ownership status of Aldersgate Capital must be determined (see Item 41).

---

## VI. Beneficial Ownership Inconsistencies

**38. The combined beneficial ownership of the two largest stockholders exceeds total preferred stock.** The beneficial ownership table (CFO memorandum Section 7.1 and management questionnaire Section 4.1) attributes 23,607,067 shares to Graymont Ventures and 15,594,267 shares to Ridgeline Growth Partners, for a combined total of 39,201,334 shares. Because these entities are venture funds that hold only preferred stock (which converts one-for-one into common stock), their combined holdings cannot exceed the 35,416,667 total shares of preferred stock outstanding. The share counts attributed to Graymont and/or Ridgeline appear to be overstated by at least 3,784,667 shares in the aggregate and must be reconciled against the capitalization records and stock transfer records.

**39. The beneficial ownership table uses inconsistent numerator and denominator bases.** The table computes each holder's percentage against a denominator of 105,366,667 fully diluted shares (which includes all 14,750,000 outstanding options and all 3,200,000 outstanding RSUs). However, the share counts attributed to individuals include only vested options and RSUs (or those vesting within 60 days), not all outstanding options and RSUs. SEC beneficial ownership disclosure generally requires the numerator and denominator to be on a consistent basis. The table should be recomputed on a consistent basis (typically, the denominator should reflect the same securities included in the numerator, plus the shares included in other holders' numerators).

**40. The founders' Class B share counts do not reconcile to their beneficial ownership share counts.** The Class B common stock holdings are stated as 12,200,000 shares (Mr. Ramaswamy) and 8,600,000 shares (Dr. Litvak), totaling 20,800,000. The beneficial ownership table attributes 12,222,533 shares to Mr. Ramaswamy and 9,693,733 shares to Dr. Litvak. The differences (22,533 and 1,093,733, respectively) presumably reflect vested options and RSUs, but the amounts should be confirmed against the equity plan records and disclosed transparently.

**41. Aldersgate Capital's current ownership is unknown.** Aldersgate Capital participated in the Series B financing (April 2020) but does not appear in any current beneficial ownership disclosure. No questionnaire respondent reports Aldersgate Capital as a current stockholder. If Aldersgate Capital holds 5% or more of any class of the Company's securities, it must be included in the principal stockholders table (Item 403 of Regulation S-K) and may be required to execute a lock-up agreement. The Company must confirm Aldersgate Capital's current holdings against the stock transfer records and obtain a completed D&O questionnaire or equivalent disclosure if Aldersgate Capital remains a 5% holder. The principal stockholders table cannot be finalized until this is resolved.

**42. The post-IPO ownership estimates in the CFO memorandum are inconsistent with the beneficial ownership share counts.** The CFO memorandum (Section 7.2) estimates post-IPO ownership of approximately 19.3% for Graymont Ventures and 12.7% for Ridgeline Growth Partners (based on 102,416,667 post-IPO shares). However, the stated share counts (23,607,067 and 15,594,267) represent 23.05% and 15.23% of 102,416,667 shares, respectively. The Section 7.2 percentages imply lower share counts (approximately 19.8 million and 13.0 million) than the Section 7.1 table states. This is consistent with the overstatement identified in Item 38 and must be reconciled.

---

## VII. Related Party and Disclosure Inconsistencies

**43. The Vantara Logistics related party transaction is inconsistently disclosed.** The Vantara Logistics, Inc. SaaS license agreement ($1.2 million annual contract value, three-year term, entered into April 2023) is a related party transaction because Vantara is a portfolio company of Graymont Ventures, whose Managing Partner, Priya Venkatesh, serves on the Company's board and on Vantara's board. Ms. Venkatesh disclosed this transaction in her director questionnaire. The CFO did not identify it as a related party transaction in his questionnaire, and the CFO memorandum (Exhibit B) and the roadshow presentation (Slide 14) present Vantara as an ordinary customer (including as a customer case study) without any related-party notation. All investor-facing materials must be updated to reflect Item 404 of Regulation S-K disclosure, including the Graymont affiliation, Ms. Venkatesh's dual board roles, the Audit Committee approval, and Ms. Venkatesh's recusal. The Form S-1 includes the Item 404 disclosure.

**44. The Vantara transaction may require lender notification under the term loan.** The term loan agreement (Section 7.2) requires prior written notification to the lender for affiliate transactions exceeding $500,000 in value. The Vantara SaaS agreement has an annual contract value of $1.2 million, which exceeds this threshold. The Company should confirm whether the required lender notification was provided and, if not, whether a cure is needed. This should be disclosed if material.

---

## VIII. Timing Inconsistencies

**45. The Form S-1 filing date is inconsistent.** The audited financial statements (Note 17, Subsequent Events) state that "in February 2025, the Company filed a Registration Statement on Form S-1 with the Securities and Exchange Commission." The CFO memorandum (dated February 14, 2025) states that the Company "anticipate[s] filing the initial Form S-1 in March 2025," and the underwriting term sheet and roadshow presentation are dated March 2025. The Form S-1 cannot have been filed in February 2025 if the target filing date is March 2025. The subsequent events note must be corrected to reflect the actual filing date (or intended filing date), and the auditor's report date must be consistent.

**46. The auditor's report date is inconsistent with the stated filing date.** The auditor's report is dated "March __, 2025." The subsequent events note states that the Form S-1 was filed in February 2025 and that subsequent events were evaluated through "March __, 2025." An auditor's report in a registration statement must be dated no later than the date of the registration statement. If the registration statement is filed in February 2025, a March-dated audit report is inconsistent. The audit report date, the subsequent events evaluation date, and the filing date must be aligned.

---

## IX. Use of Proceeds Inconsistencies

**47. The use of proceeds allocation is inconsistent across documents.** The CFO memorandum (Section 3.2) specifies a particular allocation: approximately 35% to R&D, 25% to sales and marketing, 15% to debt repayment, 10% to acquisitions, and the remainder to general corporate purposes. The roadshow presentation (Slide 27) specifies a different allocation: approximately 40% to R&D, 30% to sales and marketing, 15% to acquisitions, 10% to working capital/general corporate purposes, and 5% to term loan repayment. The underwriting term sheet (Section 4) states that the Company "has not determined the specific allocation of net proceeds among these uses." For an emerging growth company, a general use of proceeds disclosure is permissible, but the inconsistent percentages across the Company's materials must be reconciled, and the Form S-1 should not state specific allocations that the Company has not in fact determined.

**48. The pro forma as-adjusted balance sheet and dilution analysis do not reflect the stated debt repayment.** The CFO memorandum's pro forma as-adjusted capitalization table (Exhibit A) and dilution analysis (Section 8) assume the full $331.8 million of net proceeds is added to net tangible book value, with the $40.0 million term loan remaining outstanding. If a portion of the proceeds is in fact used to repay the term loan (15% per the CFO memorandum, or 5% per the roadshow), the pro forma as-adjusted balance sheet and the dilution analysis should reflect the reduced cash and reduced debt. The current presentation is internally inconsistent with the stated use of proceeds.

---

## X. Governance, Controlled Company, and Structural Risks

**49. The Board has not formally determined whether to rely on the controlled company exemption.** Following the IPO, the co-founders will hold approximately 71.8% of the voting power, making the Company a "controlled company" under Nasdaq Listing Rule 5615(c). No board resolution is on record regarding whether the Company will rely on the controlled company exemptions (from the majority-independent-board, independent-compensation-committee, and independent-nominating-committee requirements). The director questionnaire responses reflect awareness but no definitive election. As of the date of the questionnaires, only three of six (or three of seven, depending on the resolution of Item 32) directors are independent. The Board must adopt a formal resolution, and the Form S-1 must disclose whether the Company will rely on the exemption and the resulting governance implications. The Form S-1 risk factors and corporate governance disclosure assume the exemption will be relied upon, subject to Board confirmation.

**50. The dual-class structure has no sunset provision.** The Class B common stock automatically converts into Class A common stock only upon transfer to a non-permitted transferee or upon the death or incapacity of the holder. There is no time-based sunset (e.g., a fixed number of years after the IPO) and no ownership-percentage-based sunset. The dual-class structure and the resulting concentration of voting control may therefore persist indefinitely. This is disclosed as a risk factor in the Form S-1. The Company should consider whether to adopt a sunset provision, as institutional investors and proxy advisors increasingly favor sunsets, and certain index providers exclude multi-class structures.

**51. The federal forum selection clause for Securities Act claims should be disclosed.** The charter (Article X, Section 10.2) designates the federal district courts as the exclusive forum for Securities Act claims. This provision is enforceable under Delaware law following *Salzberg v. Sciabacucchi* (Del. 2020), but it affects the ability of stockholders to bring Securities Act claims (including Section 11 claims) in state court, which under *Cyan Inc. v. Beaver County Employees Retirement Fund* (2018) would otherwise be concurrently available in state and federal court. This is disclosed as a risk factor in the Form S-1.

**52. The anti-takeover provisions should be disclosed.** The charter and bylaws contain provisions that may discourage, delay, or prevent a change of control, including: a classified (staggered) board; the inability of Class A holders to act by written consent; the inability of stockholders to call special meetings; board-filled vacancies; removal of directors only for cause and only by a 66 2/3% vote; and supermajority (66 2/3%) voting requirements to amend certain provisions of the charter and bylaws. These are disclosed as risk factors in the Form S-1.

**53. The corporate opportunities renunciation should be disclosed.** The charter (Article XI) renounces the Company's interest in business opportunities presented to directors who are partners or employees of investment funds, expressly including Graymont Ventures and Ridgeline Growth Partners and their affiliates and portfolio companies. This creates a potential conflict of interest given that two directors are affiliated with these funds and that the Company has an existing related party customer relationship with a Graymont portfolio company (Vantara). This should be disclosed in the Form S-1.

---

## XI. Litigation, Tax, and Other Risks

**54. The Optivance patent litigation could trigger a term loan default.** The Optivance litigation (Case No. 6:24-cv-00831, E.D. Tex.) carries an estimated exposure of $5.0 million to $20.0 million in the event of an adverse judgment. Under the term loan agreement (Section 8.1(h)), one or more judgments for the payment of money in excess of $5.0 million in the aggregate that remain undischarged for 60 days constitute an event of default. An adverse judgment at the upper end of the range could therefore result in acceleration of the term loan if not bonded, appealed, or stayed. This is disclosed as a risk factor in the Form S-1.

**55. The Section 382 NOL limitation risk should be disclosed.** As of December 31, 2024, the Company had approximately $228.0 million of federal NOL carryforwards and $166.0 million of state NOL carryforwards. The Company has not completed a Section 382 study. The IPO and other equity transactions could trigger an "ownership change" under Section 382 of the Internal Revenue Code, which would limit the Company's ability to use its NOLs to offset future taxable income. This is disclosed as a risk factor in the Form S-1.

**56. The term loan is secured by substantially all of the Company's assets, including its intellectual property.** The first-priority lien covers substantially all assets, including the 47 issued U.S. patents, 12 pending applications, 8 foreign patents, and the registered trademarks, as well as 65% of the voting equity of the UK subsidiary. The encumbrance of the Company's core intellectual property is a significant risk and is disclosed in the Form S-1.

**57. The term loan's key-person and change-of-control provisions should be disclosed.** The term loan provides that a change of control occurs if Arun Ramaswamy ceases to serve as CEO (unless a successor acceptable to the lender is appointed within 90 days) and if existing stockholders cease to hold at least 50% of voting power (including following an IPO). While the IPO as structured does not trigger the voting-power threshold (the co-founders retain 71.8%), the loss of Mr. Ramaswamy as CEO could trigger a default. This is disclosed in the Form S-1.

**58. The 30-day pre-offering notice to the lender must be satisfied.** The term loan agreement (Section 7.14) requires the Company to provide the lender with written notice not less than 30 days before the consummation of the IPO, including the anticipated date, gross proceeds, use of proceeds, and a pro forma capitalization table. Failure to provide this notice is an event of default (subject to a 10-business-day cure). This notice must be delivered on the offering timeline and is disclosed in the Form S-1.

**59. The accumulated deficit and stockholders' deficit should be disclosed as risks.** As of December 31, 2024, the Company had an accumulated deficit of $312.6 million and a total stockholders' deficit of $288.0 million. Although the auditor issued an unqualified opinion and management believes existing liquidity is sufficient for at least 12 months, the Company's history of losses and capital needs are disclosed as risk factors.

**60. Customer concentration should be disclosed.** The Company's largest customer, Harmon Industries Inc., represented approximately 8.2% of fiscal year 2024 revenue, and the top 10 customers represented 31.7%. This concentration is disclosed as a risk factor in the Form S-1.

---

## XII. Open Items Requiring Follow-Up Before Filing

**61. Director ages must be obtained.** The management questionnaire does not provide ages for the non-employee directors (Venkatesh, Moreno, Park, Kwon, and Obi), each shown as "[●]." Item 401 of Regulation S-K requires disclosure of the age of each director and executive officer. The ages must be confirmed.

**62. Lock-up agreements must bind the fund-level entities.** The shares beneficially owned by Graymont Ventures and Ridgeline Growth Partners are held at the fund level (Graymont Ventures Fund III, L.P. and Fund IV, L.P.; Ridgeline Growth Partners Fund V, L.P.), not in the individual names of the directors who serve as their representatives. The lock-up agreements must be executed by the fund-level entities, not merely by Ms. Venkatesh and Mr. Kwon in their individual capacities. The underwriting agreement and lock-up letter forms must be cross-checked to ensure proper coverage.

**63. The underwriting discount split is undetermined.** The underwriting term sheet (Section 2) leaves the split of the underwriting discount between Pinnacle Ridge Capital and Stonebridge Greer & Co. as "[●]% (to be agreed)." This must be finalized and must comply with FINRA Rule 5110.

**64. D&O insurance must be placed.** The CFO memorandum (action item 9) notes that directors' and officers' liability insurance with appropriate public company coverage limits must be placed. This is a condition to closing and a Nasdaq listing consideration.

**65. Principal accountant fees must be obtained.** The source documents do not provide the audit, audit-related, tax, and other fees paid to Archer Pryce LLP. These amounts are required for the "Principal Accountant Fees and Services" disclosure (Item 9 of Regulation S-K) and must be obtained from the Company.

**66. The 2024 Equity Incentive Plan and ESPP documents must be filed as exhibits and reviewed.** The 2024 Equity Incentive Plan (including the double-trigger change-of-control acceleration and the 2017 Plan roll-over) and the 2024 ESPP (including the evergreen provision and the Section 423 compliance features) must be filed as exhibits and reviewed for consistency with the Form S-1 disclosures. The RSU liquidity-event (double-trigger) settlement condition should be disclosed, as it affects the timing of share settlements.

**67. The Investor Rights Agreement registration rights must be disclosed.** The Investor Rights Agreement (June 2023) provides demand registration rights and S-3 piggyback registration rights commencing 180 days after the IPO. These registration rights create potential future selling pressure and should be disclosed in the Form S-1.

**68. India operations should be confirmed.** The Company maintains an office in Bangalore, India, with employees, but the audited financial statements identify only Crestline Software UK Ltd. as a consolidated subsidiary. The Company should confirm whether there is an Indian subsidiary or branch that should be consolidated or otherwise disclosed, and whether any additional tax or regulatory disclosures are required.

**69. Interim financial statements may be required.** The audited financial statements cover fiscal years 2023 and 2024 (two years), which is sufficient for an emerging growth company. However, if the offering is not completed before the end of the first quarter of fiscal year 2025, interim financial statements for the period ended March 31, 2025 (and the comparable prior-year period) will be required, and the prospectus will need to be updated. The offering timeline should be managed with this in mind.

**70. Emerging growth company status and accommodations should be confirmed and disclosed.** The Company represents that it qualifies as an emerging growth company. With fiscal year 2024 revenue of $187.3 million (below the $1.235 billion threshold) and this being its first registered equity offering, the Company appears to qualify. The Form S-1 should disclose the EGC accommodations the Company intends to elect (including reduced executive compensation disclosure, exemption from SOX 404(b) auditor attestation, and the extended transition period for new accounting standards).

---

## XIII. Summary of Priority

The following items are the highest priority for resolution before the Form S-1 is filed, because they affect the accuracy of the audited financial statements, the correctness of named persons and entities, or the Company's compliance with its debt covenants and SEC disclosure requirements:

- Items 10, 16, and 17 (Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation error; term loan cash-flow/balance-sheet reconciliation; term loan amortization) — these affect the accuracy of the financial statements and non-GAAP disclosure and may require involvement of Archer Pryce LLP.
- Items 19, 20, 21, 24, 25, 26, and 27 (409A valuation history and equity award reconciliation) — these affect the stock-based compensation footnote and must be reconciled among the auditor, the valuation firm, and the equity plan records.
- Items 31, 32, 33, 34, and 35 (name, board size, transfer agent, and grantee identity inconsistencies) — these affect the correctness of the registration statement and the persons signing it.
- Items 38, 41, and 42 (beneficial ownership reconciliation and Aldersgate Capital) — these affect the principal stockholders table, which cannot be finalized until resolved.
- Items 43 and 44 (Vantara related party disclosure and lender notification) — these affect Item 404 disclosure and term loan compliance.
- Items 45 and 46 (filing date and auditor's report date) — these affect the timing and validity of the registration statement.
- Item 49 (controlled company exemption election) — this requires a formal board resolution and drives the corporate governance disclosure.
- Item 58 (30-day lender notice) — this is a covenant obligation on the offering timeline.

We are available to discuss any of the foregoing and to assist in their resolution.

Respectfully submitted,

[Drafting Counsel]", "file_path": "issues-memorandum.md"}

# PROSPECTUS

## CRESTLINE SOFTWARE, INC.

\[●\] shares of Class A common stock

This is the initial public offering of shares of Class A common stock of Crestline Software, Inc. We are offering \[●\] shares of Class A common stock. Prior to this offering, there has been no public market for our common stock. It is currently estimated that the initial public offering price per share will be between \$22.00 and \$26.00. We intend to list our Class A common stock on the Nasdaq Global Select Market under the ticker symbol "CRST."

We have two classes of common stock: Class A common stock and Class B common stock. The rights of the holders of Class A common stock and Class B common stock are identical, except with respect to voting and conversion. Each share of Class A common stock is entitled to one vote per share. Each share of Class B common stock is entitled to ten votes per share and is convertible at any time into one share of Class A common stock. Shares of Class B common stock will be held exclusively by our co-founders, Arun Ramaswamy and Katerina Litvak, and will automatically convert into Class A common stock upon transfer to a non-permitted transferee or upon the death or incapacity of the holder. Upon the closing of this offering, our co-founders will collectively hold approximately 71.8% of the voting power of our outstanding capital stock and, accordingly, we will be a "controlled company" under the corporate governance rules of the Nasdaq Stock Market. See "Risk Factors" and "Description of Capital Stock."

**Investing in our Class A common stock involves a high degree of risk. See "Risk Factors" beginning on page \[●\].**

Neither the Securities and Exchange Commission nor any state securities commission has approved or disapproved of these securities or determined if this prospectus is truthful or complete. Any representation to the contrary is a criminal offense.
